I proposed that the Onmi Head depicts "nothing" to do with the airplane.  I argued that the only thing "the needle" illustrates is the location of the radial, not the plane.  What followed was actually comical.  Larry, a moderately sized man but a giant in terms of experience and practical flying knowledge, stopped in mid-thought while we were flying.  After we landed, I expected a run-down on why I had blown the flight exam (which Larry was famous for).  Instead, he grabbed my Log Book and signed me off.  Then, he sat for several minutes and drew diagrams of the Onmi Head and its physics.  He concluded my argument was valid ... not because I was smart (which I'm not) but because It's the way I related to the visual depiction of the instrument.  As long as it worked for me, and was technically correct, Larry let me cling to my assessment.  I will "never" forget this man who helped so many people achieve the thrill of flying and know they had earned it when "LARRY BARTLETT" signed their Log Book.  Postscript: If you were taking a Flight Exam for an Instrument or Commercial Rating from Larry ... it was wise for the uninitiated to pack a role of toilet paper for the test.

The other transforms a commercial airliner into a luxury penthouse in the clouds. The Gulfream G700 and the Airbus A220 showcase two entirely distinct philosophies of airborne luxury. In this video, we're examining range, interior space, advanced systems, and actual ownership costs. One of these aircraft offers capabilities the other cannot match, regardless of budget. Welcome to Alto Lux. Let's begin with what truly counts, the interior. At the end of the day, you're not purchasing engines or aerodynamics. You're investing in an environment, a private domain, a retreat at 45,000 ft. The Gulfream G700 provides an interior length of 56 ft and 9 in. Width 8 ft and 2 in, height 6 ft and 3 in. These dimensions might not appear groundbreaking until you experience it firsthand. This represents the tallest, widest, and longest interior Gulfream has ever produced. You can stand completely upright. You can move freely. You can genuinely forget you're aboard an aircraft. The G700 usually seats up to 19 passengers in its typical layout, though capacity increases if you're prepared to sacrifice some luxury space. But here's where it becomes fascinating. The A220 Executive Jet delivers interior dimensions that make the G7000 appear relatively compact. We're discussing an interior length reaching 99 ft. Width 11 ft and 2 in. Height 7 ft and 1 in. Flight deck. Now, let's examine the front of the aircraft, the flight deck. The Gulfream G700 features a symmetry flight deck, which represents Gulfream's most sophisticated cockpit technology. You're seeing 10 touchscreens, active control side sticks, and the most userfriendly avionics package in executive aviation. The predictive landing performance technology provides pilots realtime runway data. The enhanced flight vision technology combines infrared and synthetic vision capabilities. The A220 Executive Jet alternatively comes equipped with a Pratt and Whitney Pure Power engines controlled through a sophisticated commercial-grade flight deck. The cockpit utilizes the Rockwell Collins Proline Fusion avionics package. It's proven technology. The same systems operating hundreds of commercial A220 globally every single day. That ensures exceptional reliability. It also means your pilot availability is potentially greater since more pilots hold type ratings on Airbus aircraft than on Gulf Streams. But here's where it becomes nuanced. The G700's flight deck is designed specifically for executive aviation operations. Smaller airfields, more challenging approaches, quick repositioning. The A22O's cockpit is designed for airline operations. Reliability, efficiency, standardization. Neither is superior. They're optimized for different purposes. Engine and performance. This is where specifications become reality, or rather where the turbine meets the atmosphere. The Gulfream G700 is powered by two Rolls-Royce Pearl 700 engines. Each generates 18,850 lb of thrust. Maximum speed, MAC 0.925, which equals roughly 710 mph. But speed alone doesn't reveal everything. The G700 has a maximum range of 7,500 nautical miles. That enables New York to Tokyo non-stop, Los Angeles to London without refueling, Singapore to Los Angeles on a single tank. The service ceiling reaches 51,000 ft, enabling you to fly above weather and congestion. The Airbus A22 Executive Jet utilizes two Pratt and Whitney PW 15000G engines. These are geared turboan engines. Extremely efficient, remarkably quiet. Maximum cruise speed around 520 mph. That's noticeably slower than the G700. Maximum range 3,500 nautical miles in its standard configuration, though extended range versions can reach 5,600 nautical miles. Service ceiling 41,000 ft. Here's what these numbers mean practically. The G700 gets you there quicker and can fly farther without stopping. If your mission involves intercontinental travel, if you're frequently crossing the Atlantic or Pacific, the G700 is engineered for that purpose. The A220 Executive Jet excels at regional travel and medium-range journeys. Paris to Dubai, perfect. Miami to S. Paulo, absolutely. But Sydney to Los Angeles, you'll require a fuel stop. investment. Let's discuss finances. The uncomfortable topic nobody wants to address, but everyone considers. The Gulfream G700 carries a list price of approximately $75 million. Pre-owned examples don't really exist yet since deliveries are recent. Charter rates typically range between $8,000 and $12,000 per flight hour depending on route and availability. The Airbus A220 Executive Jet. The base aircraft costs approximately $81 million. But here's the catch. That's just the green aircraft. The completion, all that custom interior work, those showers, those lounges can easily add another $30 to $50 million. You're realistically considering 110 to 130 million for a fully customized A220 executive jet. Charter rates vary significantly depending on configuration, but typically start around $15,000 per flight hour. So, here's that surprising feature I mentioned. The Gulfream G700 includes something called the Gulfream cabin management system with cabin altitude control that maintains the cabin pressure equivalent to 3,900 ft. Even when flying at 51,000 ft, the A220, its cabin altitude at cruise is typically around 6,000 ft. That difference might seem minor, but it has a significant impact on how you feel after a long flight. Lower cabin altitude means better oxygen saturation, reduce fatigue, and quicker recovery from jet lag. It's a detail most people never think about, but your body definitely notices it. The G700 is the precision instrument, precise, purpose-built, optimized for the mission it was designed to accomplish. The A220 Executive Jet is the versatile solution. Versatile, spacious, capable of configurations that simply aren't feasible in any purpose-built executive jet. Which one is worthwhile? That depends entirely on your requirements. If you're flying long range routes frequently, if speed matters, if you value that refined executive jet experience, the G700 is worth every dollar of its $75 million price tag. But if you need space, if you're transporting larger groups, if you want a flying office that doubles as a bedroom and triples as a conference center, the A220 Executive Jet justifies its higher cost. - Pilatus PC-12 Condition Lever: Kind: captions Language: en all right hey guys welcome back i've gotten a couple questions about the fuel condition lever in the pc12 so i thought i'd jump into x-plane here and make a quick video about the condition lever down here what it's used for and how it works there seems to be a little bit of confusion about this because it does look kind of like the mixture knob in a piston airplane however it works completely differently the only similarity between the two is that when you bring this lever all the way to the aft stop it does shut down the engine but that's about where the similarities end so this lever basically just controls the idle speed of the engine and there are three detents there's cut off feather ground idle and flight idle when we first get into the airplane this lever will be in cut-off feather the engine will be shut down and when we go to start the engine we'll use the electric starter to turn the engine and once we have enough rotational speed we'll introduce fuel so we're looking at the ng up here and if you haven't seen my video about starting the pc12 i'll link to that so you can check that out but basically how we start any turbine engine is we just get the engine spinning and then we want it to spin up fast enough to where it's sucking in enough air that when we introduce fuel that we can have a light off on the engine and it will spool up and become the process becomes self-sustaining when we're starting the engine we want to see 13 to 18 percent ng we really want it to be as high as it can go to light the engine off usually it'll top out somewhere between 18 19 or 20 percent in g right here this ng is just the rotational speed of the engine the rpm and the engine expressed in a percentage of its maximum rotation it can go a little over 100 but this is primarily what we're looking at here when we're dealing with the condition lever so uh once we get that 13 to 18 percent uh ng during the the start sequence we'll introduce uh fuel by moving this lever out of the cutoff feather detent up and over a gate and into the ground idle detent and the sim doesn't have this modeled exactly correctly in the rail airplane there's actually a black tab right here that's a guard that's spring loaded to the left and you've got it move that to the right with your thumb when you're bringing this lever aft it's not a problem when you're moving it forward but the guard is there to prevent an accidental shutoff in flight but that tab isn't even modeled in here at all but at any rate you do still have to lift this lever up and over a gate out of the cut off feather position and into the ground idle position and it'll just it'll seat down into that detent with a nice solid click and that will introduce fuel and then light the engine off during the start sequence at ground idle the ng should be somewhere between about 63 or 65 percent and the sim it's not modeled exactly correctly it's a little bit lower than that as you can see here and then the reason that we we keep the engine in ground idle on the ground is because it keeps the engine running smoothly it keeps the propeller in the approved rpm it turns the generators fast enough to supply electrical current to the components we need but we're not wasting fuel by turning the engine faster than that because we don't really have much of a need for bleed air on the ground now once we take off we'll move the condition lever up here into flight idle and again there is a detent that it'll seed into in flight idle as we're sliding that lever forward we don't have to um move up out of a gate or anything we just push it forward and it'll seat into the flight idle detent and that'll bring the ng somewhere up to between 75 or 80 percent again it's a little bit lower than that in the sim it's not quite realistic but somewhat close and that'll just spool the engine up and uh the reason that we want the condition lever and flight idle between any time between takeoff and landing so the entire time that the airplane's in the air is because we really want to have more bleed air for pressurization and for the de-ice boots if we need those while we're flying so you know remember that when the engine is is turning it's ingesting air mostly for combustion but it's also some of that is getting routed away from the engine for bleed air as bleed air that'll come into the cabin and pump up the cabin it'll pump up the ice boots if we need to run those so we need a lot of bleed air in flight so that's why we don't want the engine to idle too slowly otherwise we won't get enough bleed air so the only thing the condition lever does is it controls the idle position of the engine so if i have the pcl which is the power control lever the throttle right here at the idle stop like it is right now the idle ng will not go lower than where it's set based on the condition lever position so if i'm in flight idle i can bring the pcl all the way back to its stop and the engine should not spool down below about 75 or 80 percent of g so i'm going to have plenty of bleed air and generator current and all the stuff that i need it's also handy in flight because especially during approach and landing when we're using lower power settings if we have to go around say we're on short final and aircraft taxis out under the runway in front of us and we need to make a quick go around you really want the engine to be spooled already so that we don't have that lag time there's a little bit of spool time even in a smaller turbine engine like this and we don't really want to have to wait on the engine to spool up to get that power so it's nice to have the engine already pre-spooled and and have that power be readily available should be needed so as soon as we land and we're out of reverse if we needed to use reverse and we're almost slowed down to taxi speed and we don't need the engine spooled up anymore we'll bring that back to ground idle we do have to lift the condition lever slightly up and forward out of the detent and back and let it click down into the ground idle detent and then we'll taxi into the ramp at ground idle and then when we're ready to shut the engine down got to make sure it's been at least two minutes for the hot section to cool down to the engine but then we'll at that point shut the engine down by just bringing this condition lever up we pull it up push the tab that again isn't modeled here in the sim but push that to the right with the thumb and then just pull that all the way back and seat it into the cutoff feather position and that does three things first it chokes the engine to fuel to shut it down it de-energizes the low fuel pressure system so that we're not seeing a nuisance cast or cause light when we're shutting the engine down and it also feathers the prop which is where we want it for shutdown so that's really all it does it just basically controls the idle uh idle speed of the engine and that's what we use it for so in the in the ngx it's full fedex uh the condition lever is actually gone it's all integrated into the pcl which is pretty pretty handy dandy so anyway hope that clears up the questions guys thanks for those uh leave me your questions in the comments don't forget to like and subscribe and i will see you guys on the next video thanks for watching

- What You Need to Know About the Daher TBM 960: Kind: captions Language: en This isn't a jet, but it'll fly like one. The TBM960 is a single engine turborop that's rewriting the rule book on what personal aviation can look like. It's faster than some light jets, climbs like a rocket, and lands on short strips with ease. All while being operated by a single pilot. But here's the real twist. Behind this sleek machine lies a level of automation and performance that's got even jet owners turning their heads. We're diving into everything from cabin to cockpit, engine specs to realworld numbers, and finally, what this aircraft really costs to own and operate. Let's get into the TBM960. Cabin. The TBM960 delivers a cabin experience that borders on executive class comfort with smart engineering behind every inch. The layout offers four leather seats in a club configuration with two pilot seats up front. Optional fifth or sixth seating can be added with a belted lavatory seat or rear bench. The cabin measures about 4 ft wide and just over 4 ft tall, offering good headroom for this class of aircraft. Soundproofing is where it gets impressive. Thanks to a fiveblade composite propeller and redesigned engine mounts, in-flight noise is remarkably low. Passengers often describe the TBM as quiet enough for conversation without headsets. Each seat has USB charging, cup holders, and LED lighting. Foldable tray tables allow passengers to work or dine in flight. The pressurization system keeps cabin altitude at just 6,000 ft, even when cruising at 28,000, which significantly reduces fatigue. Cargo space includes a rear cabin storage zone and an external baggage compartment that offers over 30 cubic feet of volume. Ideal for golf bags, duffles, or equipment. Interior upgrades include reclining seats, Alcantara headliners, wood veneer trim, and climate controls. The overall design feels premium but purpose-built. This is more than just a personal airplane. Many owners use it for business travel, corporate shuttles, or medical flights. It's a flying office, and it's surprisingly comfortable. Another small luxury, the cabin lighting system. It includes both ambient and directional LEDs, dimmable from the main panel or individual seat controls. You can set it to match the time of day, which helps combat jet lag on longer flights. The climate system, too, is smart. It uses a digital controller to manage both heating and cooling. And it's split between cabin and cockpit zones, so the pilot can stay cool while the passengers stay warm. Cockpit. In the cockpit, the TBM960 offers jet level avionics in a setup that's optimized for solo operation. You'll find the Garmin G3000 avionic suite front and center with three highresolution displays and touchscreen controllers. It includes synthetic vision, weather radar, realtime traffic, terrain avoidance, and envelope protection. Workload is lightened by intelligent features like electronic stability protection, emergency descent mode, and automatic fuel tank switching. One standout is auto level. If you become disoriented, a single button brings the aircraft back to straight and level flight. But the crown jewel, Garmin's Homesafe Autoland system. If the pilot is incapacitated, a passenger can hit one button. The system picks the best nearby airport, flies the approach, lands the plane, stops on the runway, and even shuts down the engine. The ergonomic layout makes everything feel intuitive. There's no mixture or prop control, just a single power lever thanks to the fully digital FADEC system. Push forward for full power. Pull back to descend. That's it. Flying the TBM feels more like operating a system than a machine. And that's a good thing when you're doing it alone at 31,000 ft. Another rarely mentioned perk, in-flight connectivity. While not standard, you can equip the TBM with a satellite-based Aridium data link or even Wi-Fi for basic communications. It's not streaming level internet, but for email and weather updates at 30,000 ft, it gets the job done. And yes, all of this might sound like it comes with jet level expenses, but wait until you hear how its cost per hour stacks up against some business jets fuel bills alone. Now, let's dig into what this machine actually delivers in the sky. Engine and performance. The TBM 960 is powered by a Pratt and Whitney PT 6E66 XT engine producing about 700 shaft horsepower. This engine is managed entirely by FADEC, meaning no prop lever, no mixture, just one digital throttle for everything. Climb rate is a key highlight. The aircraft can reach 31,000 ft in less than 19 minutes. And it does so with remarkable smoothness. At cruise, the 960 flies at 370 mph true air speed, comparable to many light jets. Even at that speed, fuel burn stays efficient, about 55 gall in high-speed cruise. That's less than half what many jets burn. In economy cruise, you'll get a range of roughly 1,900 statute miles with IFR reserves, easily covering non-stop trips like Los Angeles to Chicago or Miami to Boston. Takeoff distance is around 2,300 ft. Landing distance about 1,900 ft. That opens up access to thousands of small runways across North America, places jets simply can't go. Handling is smooth and forgiving. Pilots love the stability, especially in turbulence. Thanks to the fiveblade prop and aerodynamic refinements, the TBM feels agile, but never twitchy. Another key point, performance at high elevation airports. Whether you're flying out of Aspen, Colorado, or Telleluride, the TBM maintains excellent climb and takeoff performance, even on hot days. That hot and high capability makes it ideal for mountainous regions. Maintenance intervals are long, and the PT6 engine series has a reputation for near bulletproof reliability. With over 500 million hours of operation across the PT6 family, it's arguably the most trusted turbine in aviation history. And here's something numbers can't quite explain, the handling feel. The TBM flies like it's on rails, responsive without being twitchy. Control forces are firm but natural. Many pilots say it feels like a military trainer crossed with a business jet. So yes, it's got one engine, but that engine delivers performance that rivals jets with two. Price. Here's where things get interesting and for many prospective buyers, gamechanging. The base price of the TBM960 starts at just under $5 million US, but most fully equipped models with options like upgraded interiors, satellite connectivity, and advanced avionics will push the final price closer to $5.9 million. Now, that might seem steep until you compare it to a light jet like the Citation M2 or the Honda Jet. Both cost around $6 to7 million, and neither can match the TBM's combination of range, speed, and operating efficiency. Speaking of operating costs, on average, direct operating cost is around $800 to $1,000 per hour. That includes fuel, maintenance, engine reserves, and parts. Insurance, hanger, and pilot costs vary, but annual fixed costs typically land in the $30 to $50,000 range. There's also one massive cost advantage. The TBM960 requires only one pilot. That alone can save you over $100,000 per year if you're flying commercially or with crew. Depreciation is relatively stable, too. TBMs have a strong resale value thanks to their performance, build quality, and loyal owner community. And because the 960 is so new, demand on the secondhand market is already high. If you're upgrading from a piston aircraft or downsizing from a jet, the TBM offers a unique middle ground. All the performance you want with operating costs that won't bankrupt your balance sheet. So, yes, this turborop may not have two engines, but its price toerformance ratio leaves some jets in the dust.

The cockpit ergonomics are designed with pilots in mind. The controls are intuitively laid out, making it easy to operate the aircraft even during critical phases of flight. This means you can focus on flying safely while having confidence in your ability to manage the aircraft's systems. Safety is, of course, a top priority in the cockpit as well. It is equipped with a comprehensive set of safety features, including advanced weather radar, terrain awareness and warning systems, and traffic collision avoidance systems. These systems provide you with the information you need to make informed decisions and avoid potential hazards. Before we take a look at the Engine & Performance and of course the cost of this amazing jet, make sure you hit that like and subscribe button for more interesting videos in the world of aviation. With over 1,825 horsepower, the engine packs a serious punch, allowing this aircraft to reach impressive speeds and climb rates. And speaking of speed, the 960 is no slouch in that department. So, whether you're on a short hop or a cross-country journey, this plane gets you to your destination in no time. But it's not just about speed; it's also about efficiency. The PT6A engine is designed to sip fuel rather than guzzle it. This means you can cover long distances without worrying too much about refueling stops. The engine's efficiency also translates to reduced operating costs, making it a smart choice for both personal and business travel. Now, let's talk about the range. This plane has an impressive range that allows you to connect cities that are thousands of miles apart without breaking a sweat. With a maximum range of over 1900 miles, this aircraft lets you explore new horizons without the need for constant refueling. Climbing capabilities? The TBM 960 has those covered too. With its powerful engine and aerodynamic design, this aircraft can climb to altitudes of 31,000 feet in just about 18 minutes. That means you can quickly get above turbulent weather and enjoy a smooth and comfortable ride. - Skipped Checklists, Fatal Crash | King Air 350 Addison: Kind: captions Language: en Addison Airport, Texas. This King Air 350 was just about to depart for Florida. On board, there were eight passengers and two flight crew. And very shortly, this flight was going to be in chaos. The following video will explain what happens during this fateful flight and how it was able to happen. On the 30th of June 2019, this Textron Aviation B300, also known as a King Air 350, was due to fly from Texas to Albert Witted Airport, St. Petersburg in Florida. The flight was scheduled for around 0900 Central Daylight Time with the pilots and passengers arriving at the airport hanger around 90 minutes before. Earlier in the day, the aircraft had been refueled with a total of 329 gallons of fuel in preparation for the flight with no maintenance taking place. When the passengers arrived, the co-pilot greeted the passengers in the hanger and loaded the bags into the baggage compartment. This was done without any scales present with none of the bags being weighed before being put onto the plane. The pilots and co-pilot then carried out a walkound of the aircraft to ensure everything was in order for their trip. With both pilots happy, they assisted the passengers onto the aircraft and then they boarded the aircraft themselves. The pilot flying was very experienced with 16,450 total flying hours with 1,100 hours on the King Air 350. He was 71 years old with a class one medical certification with waiverss. The co-pilots had 2,357 total flying hours. He was 28 years old with a class one medical certification. Before starting the aircraft, they obtained the most up-to-date weather from the automatic terminal information system or ATIS. This informed them that the wind was from 10° at 6 knots. The clouds were scattered at 1400 ft. Visibility was more than 10 km. The temperature was 24° centigrade with an altimeter setting of 306 in of mercury. Shortly after this, they were ready for engine start. Now, I want you to pay attention to the times that follow as they are important to this incident. At 0903, the left engine was started. 15 seconds later, the right engine was then started. As the engines roared into life, the passengers settled into their seats for their trip across the United States. At 0905, the pilots contacted ground control and informed them that they were ready for taxi. They were subsequently provided with the taxi instructions to runway 15 and immediately began transiting there. This was only a short taxi with them arriving at the taxi holding point within a minute or so. At 0909 and 41 seconds, the local controller gave the pilot departure instructions to turn left heading 050 on departure and cleared the flight for takeoff from runway 15. With the crew ready to depart at 09, 10, and 11 seconds, the pilots move the power levers forward, setting takeoff power. Now, I tried to not interject with my own opinions during these videos, and I tried to stay within the facts known from the investigation, but I just want to add that at this point, no checklist had been used in the cockpit between the two pilots, and it had been 7 minutes from the left engine starting to the aircraft starting its takeoff roll. Even if the items in the checklist were carried out without being verbally confirmed, this would not have been sufficient time to complete all the actions. As the plane started to roll down the runway, the co-pilot called air speeds alive, which meant that the airspeed indicator was showing the air speed increasing. The cockpit remained silent for the rest of the takeoff with the pilots rotating the aircraft at 102 knots. At 0910 and 34 seconds, the King Air lifted off from the runway. 6 seconds after takeoff, the pilot stated, "What in the world?" as the engine RPM on the left engine and a little on the right started to decrease. Shortly after, the stoall warner sounded. The aircraft was still climbing, but now started to roll to the left. The nose attitude was lowered slightly, and the stoall warner stopped. The co-pilot now said to the pilot, "You just lost your left engine." The aircraft continued to roll to the left. There was a slight increase in thrust in the left engine before it started to decrease again. The aircraft now reached 100 ft above the ground and the stall warner started again along with the bank angle annunciator. The aircraft was continuing to roll to the left, now reaching a near inverted position. The bankang annunciator continued with the aircraft now at 70 ft above the ground with the ground speed of around 85 knots. At 0910 and 51 seconds, the aircraft impacted a hanger left of the runway in an inverted position. This resulted in an explosion and a fire which unfortunately led to the deaths of everyone on board. So, what actually happened to cause this crash? By the sounds of the information we already know, it looks as though the left engine failed, which caused the aircraft to lose height and crash. The problem with this hypothesis is that even if this was true, the crew should have been able to control the aircraft and fly away safely. This crash is a result of several factors, all of which contribute to the overall result. When investigators arrived on the scene and examined the wreckage, it was discovered that there was no condition that would have prevented normal operation of the engine and propellers, and it was found that both engines were operating at the point of impact. Due to the impact damage, it could not be determined whether there was a malfunction with the throttle control system, so this couldn't be ruled out. They then looked at the possibility of the power levers moving due to insufficient friction settings on the aircraft power friction locks. These friction locks, as the name suggests, increase or decrease the friction on the power levers to the pilot's desired force preference. If they are left too loose, the power levers can move with very little force. And it has been known that the vibrations caused by takeoff power can cause the power levers to shift. And in this case, it appears that as the thrust was reducing in odd steps along with the right engine losing a slight bit of thrust, this was probably the case. It would explain why the thrust was acting in the way that it did. The friction locks are set and checked in the before engine start checklist and the before takeoff or run-up checks. In this instance, no checklists were carried out. Although the co-pilot reportedly had flown with the pilots many times previously and was familiar with the B300, he was not type rated in this airplane and was not allowed to operate the flight controls when passengers were on board. Therefore, the co-pilot may not have checked or adjusted the friction settings before the flight's departure. If they had carried out all the relevant checklists, this would have ensured that the flying controls, the auto feather system, and the rudder boost system were all operating correctly before takeoff. The auto feather system allows for the propeller to feather if power is lost to an engine. This means that it will severely reduce the amount of drag on the aircraft, assisting with its rate of climb. The rudder boost system assists the pilot by reducing the required rudder pedal force in the event of an engine failure. Rudder Boost is armed by selecting the control switch to the rudder boost position. Several pilots who knew the accident pilot and flew with him in the past were interviewed. Regarding the accident pilot's takeoff rotation technique, two pilots reported that he used two hands during rotation. None of the pilots interviewed reported that the accident pilot asked them to back him up or to guard the power levers during takeoff or rotation. One pilot reported that the accident pilots had an aggressive rotation technique and that he would pull up abruptly at rotation. Another pilot reported that the accident pilot was not strong on using checklists. Another mutual acquaintance of the accident pilot and co-pilot stated that the accident pilot did not like to use the checklist and just jumped into the airplane and went. His business partner also reported that the accident pilot generally would not do a weight and balance calculation if he was familiar with the airplane and usually verbalized vspeeds. In addition to the lack of checklists, there was also not a discussion on the pilot or co-pilot's actions on an emergency. This is usually covered in a takeoff brief. Although the co-pilots verbally identified the loss of the left engine in response to the pilot's confused reaction to the airplane's performance, it is likely the co-pilot did not initiate any corrective flight control inputs. possibly due to the pilot's established practice of being the sole operator of flight controls when passengers were on board. This would also explain the silent cockpit during the takeoff role. You would expect to hear that the pilot monitoring to call out the VS speeds such as V1 and rotate. V1 refers to the points in which takeoff must be continued and rotate being the points where the aircraft is at a sufficient speed above its minimum control speed to allow for a safe departure. For this flight, the pilot rotated the aircraft at 102 knots with the actual rotate speed for the weather and weight of the day being 110 knots. This decreased the margin to the minimum controllable air speed and likely lessened the amount of time available for the pilot to properly react to the reduction in thrust and maintain aircraft control. Along with the early rotation speed, the lack of crew resource management and checklists, the aircraft was also over its maximum takeoff weight. The airplane's maximum takeoff and landing weight was £15,000. Based on the pilot's FAA records, passenger weights provided by family members, baggage, and other items recovered from the wreckage, and with the fuel on board, the aircraft's established ramp weight before departure was £15,660. Although the aircraft was slightly over its maximum takeoff weight for departure, its rate of climb was near what would be expected at maximum weight in the weather conditions of the day and therefore the weight exceeded was not likely a factor in this accident. The most important factor to analyze for this incident is the immediate actions of the crew to the issues they were having. The normal and expected actions for an engine failure on takeoff would be to keep the aircraft flying straight with the rudder and maybe a bit of aeron. Get the gear up once you have a positive rate of climb and the flaps up if you're using them. One really important factor with prop aircraft is to ensure that the failed engine has feathered. What is meant by this is that once the engine stops producing thrust, the blades are moved to a near 90° position to reduce the amount of drag. If the propeller is left unfathered after an engine failure, the additional drag on the aircraft is tremendous. If this is not done automatically, this can be resolved by moving the propeller lever to the feathered position manually. When they investigated the position of the propeller blades, it was determined that the prop was not feathered during this incident. It was also noted that the landing gear was in the extended position and this means that the drag on the aircraft was extensive. With the reduction in thrust, this would have made it very difficult to climb or increase speed. The investigators examined how much yaw or side slip the aircraft was experiencing during the loss of thrust. Based on an evaluation of thrust estimates provided by the propeller manufacturer and performance data provided by the airplane manufacturer, it is likely that in the early stages of the incident, the pilot applied left rudder, which was the opposite input needed to maintain lateral control before then applying right rudder seconds later. Applying left rudder would have only worsened the situation increasing the your effect of the aircraft and in turn as a secondary effect adding roll to the left. Even after reverting to the correct rudder input by then the aircraft's roll rate was increasing too rapidly and its altitude was too low to recover. As always, I would never criticize the actions of pilots during a surprise and highly stressful situation. In this emergency, the pilot's experience and training should have mitigated some of the actions that were or were not carried out. However, one thing that cannot be excused in the world of aviation is where complacency takes hold and safety becomes a lesser priority. Not using checklists or even carrying out the pre-flight checks from memory should not be happening as these are used to check the aircraft is in full working order. It's much better to discover that the aircraft is faulty on the ground rather than in the middle of an emergency. I would be interested to hear what you think about this incident and look forward to reading your comments. I hope you're all having a great day and as always, I'll see you in the next one.

responsible for navigation and aircraft performance with the sensor operator and Tactical Systems operating responsible for operating the mission equipment in the back of the modified King Air their mission planning followed a standard protocol outlined in Air Force instructions and it's began with a thorough review of the mission execution forecast notices to aan which is what it was called at the time and isolated Personnel reports or ISO preps which is what they would need if they were to become isolated in the hostile environment they were flying over this ensured the crew was briefed on Focus relying heavily on their instruments to navigate through the partial cloud cover at 1229 local time the sensor operator reported that they were on station at 20,000 ft once the crew were set for endurance at around 150 knots with the autopilot on however a large rapidly buildin towering cumulus cloud dominated the center of their operating area reaching altitudes as high as 24,000 ft This Cloud was dangerous to the aircraft with the turbulence and icing associated with it and it was starting to jeopardize the success of the mission the crew was starting to experience light to moderate turbulence while flying in and out of the surrounding clouds and the crew started to discuss their options they could move from their current position but this would reduce the cover they could offer plus they would have to renegotiate clearance into the surrounding tactical airspace they could descend below the cloud but this would put them more at risk of surface toare missiles they could climb above the cloud or they could just end the mission and return early potentially jeopardizing the safety of the troops below or losing the intelligence they would gather ending the mission early was only to be used if their safety was at risk and they were only 40 minutes into their mission so had plenty of fuel to use so they decided if the cloud became more of an issue they would climb above it at 12:40 43 minutes after taking off from Kandahar the growing Cloud continued to drift into the aircraft's orbit so the mission Commander made the decision to climb from their initial altitude of 20,000 ft to 23,000 ft after checking there were no aircraft above them they were cleared to climb to 23,000 ft the pilots then changed the altitude selector and then selected vertical speed or pitch for the autopilot to follow and climb to the requested height it is worth noting at this point that there are different methods of climbing with the autopilot on the King Air 350 and many other aircraft and they differ slightly as the aircraft uses different metrics to climb this is important to what happens next the two main selections are vertical speed and flight level change when vertical speed is selected the pilot will also select a rate of climb or descent for the aircraft to follow it's the rate of climb selected that the autopilots will ensure remains are constant speed is not considered and it is the responsibility of the pilot to control as there are no Auto throttles the other selection is flight level change which will climb or descend based on the speed selected by the pilot the autopilot will then adjust the rate of climb or descent based on the speed and power selected if the speed is low the aircraft will level or enter a very shallow climb to allow for the speed to increase before then increasing the rate of climb another less use method is pitch mode which like vertical speed mode will hold a specific pitch also selected by the pilot but does not adjust the speed so in this instance the pilot either selected pitch mode or vertical speed with a 500 ft per minute rate of climb the aircraft pitched up and they started to climb to 23,000 ft still looking out for the bad weather both Pilots alternated around looking out and ensuring the aircraft was set up for its new orbit at 23,000 ft the pilot and Mission Commander discussed setting up a new orbit in the flight management system the pilot then set up the new orbit and mentioned that he would insert it once they were established on their correct level whilst they were doing this the aircraft speed was reducing just 10 seconds into the climb the pilots noticed the aircraft speed was reducing and stated a little slow correcting the pilots moved the power levers forward which started to introduce more power to the climb another effect masked by the autopilot was that when the pilot increased the power to the propellers especially at a high angle of attack the engine's torque or P factor causes the aircraft to roll and ya to the left this was masked by the autopilot that moved the Yoke into the right Bank position to compensate for this phenomenon this was very quickly becoming a very dangerous position to being they were in a climbing left turn turn with slow air speed and with the power slowly increasing causing more roll to the left the increased height also increase the danger as the risk of stall increases as the air density is reduced 7 seconds after the pilot acknowledged the reducing speed the mission Commander then stated all right firewall this meant to move the power levers fully forward for maximum power this causes the engines to push Beyond its normal range and produce 156% torque for both engines this is permitted for a limit of 20 seconds but as we previously discussed with the engine torque adding left roll to the aircraft this caused greater than normal amounts of additional left Roll Just 1 second later the autopilot which was holding the control services to the right to combat The increased torque was disengaged by the pilot by moving the Yoke to the neutral position this add a further Left Bank to the already turning aircraft at this point the aircraft speed was at 116 knots the aircraft Bank increased dramatically the video feed for the sensor operator at the back was lost indicated to the crew that they had entered an increasingly extreme altitude simultaneously the bank angle warning activated indicating that the aircraft exceeded 50° of Bank the aircraft had entered a spin and was now entering clouds with the mission Commander calling for max power before then stating all right eyes inside eyes inside despite this the aircraft Store Warner sounded shortly after over the next 7 Seconds the flight deck was consumed with attempts to stabilize the aircraft but turbulence and rapid changes in altitude caused unsecured items to begin flying around amid the chaos the pilot exclaimed whoa pull up as the aircraft Control Systems entered an emergency no computed data mode in this mode the primary flights display removes all instrument readings except for red Chevrons that directs the pilot to pull up and recover from the unusual attitude the pilot was attempting to bring the aircraft attitude back to the Horizon but it had entered a spiral descent every time he pulled back on the Yoke to reduce the rate of descent it only increased it as the stor Wing kept the aircraft rolling to the left with all the chaos in the flights deck the mission Commander remained extremely calm stating eyes inside look at your air speed the air speed was now increasing rapidly towards their max speed of 245 knots the aircraft's rate of descent was increasing Beyond 10,000 ft per minute with the aircraft passing 18,000 ft with the local ground level being around 6,000 ft above sea level they had around 12,000 ft remaining the mission Commander then stated eyes inside eyes inside my aircraft power back with the pilot stating your aircraft at this point the rate of descent had increased to over 12,000 ft per minute with the mission Commander now in control he was trying to address the issues with both air speed and stability despite these efforts the aircraft overspeed warning and landing gear horn sounded in quick succession signaling that the throttles have been reduced towards Idol and that the aircraft speed had exceeded 245 knots the cockpit voice recorder fell silent with only the AAL warnings filling the air marking the final moment of the crew's Valiant struggle to regain control at 12:43 Independence 08 reached its tragic conclusion they impacted Valley terrain approximately 110 nautical miles Northeast of candahar Airfield the aircraft struck the ground with its fuselage slightly nose down and in a Left Bank investigators noted minimal forward momentum indicating that The Descent was Steep and uncontrolled in the moments before impact the force of the crash was devastating the MC12 was obliterated upon impact with much of the structure consumed by post crash fire the only significant portion of the aircraft found away from the main impact site was the right wing tip and winglet the rest of the debris field was remarkably compact with the majority of the wreckage contained within a 50 m radius this suggested that the aircraft was in a near vertical descent corroborating findings from flight data and cockpit recordings eyewitness accounts added further detail to the scene a local National living near the crash site reported seeing the aircraft descending in a pronounced left turn according to the testimony a puff or trail of black smoke emanated from the aircraft just before it struck the ground while the origin of the smoke is unclear it suggested a catastrophic failure in the final moments possibly related to the engine strain during the climb or the extreme aerodynamic forces in the spin for the crew of independent 08 there was no chance of survival the impact and subsequent fire made the crash site a somber Memorial to the four highly skilled Airmen who dedicated themselves to their mission the loss of independent 08 initiated an immediate and coordinated search and rescue effort at 1325 local time Freedom 14 another MC12 from a nearby unit was tasked with locating the wreckage and supporting the search and rescue Operation Freedom 14 was Airborne within minutes and arrived over the crash site by 1335 on approach the crew of Freedom 14 spotted the smoldering remains of Independence 08 nestled in the valley terrain where it had impacted at 1432 a ground team from Ford operating base Warrior reached the crash site their Grim task was to recover the remains of the four crew members just over 2 hours later at 16:35 local time the ground team reported back with the somber confirmation four Heroes recovered the operation did not end there at 2130 a second ground team arrived to recover additional equipment and conduct a more detailed investigation the crash site posed significant security risks due to its remote location and the ongoing threat of insurgent activity to prevent sensitive technology from falling into the wrong hands the decision was made to destroy the remaining wreckage in place after the second team completed their mission explosives were used to ensure the debris could not be salvaged or exploited by hostile forces the operation underscored the professionalism and determination by Coalition forces to respond swiftly to crisis even in the most challenging circumstances the recovery of the falling Airmen and the protection of sensitive materials from the crash site were achieved with Precision ensuring the legacy of independent 08 crew remained untarnished by their tragic loss the accident investigation board noted that the cause of the mishap was a stall due to ins efficient air speed while in a climbing left turn which developed into a left spin followed quickly by a highspeed spiral from which the crew was unable to recover additionally each of the following three factors substantially contributed to the mishap the orbit weather had impeded visibility and masked The Horizon Pilots inexperience in the MC12 and known MC12 program risks associated with sustaining required combat capability in theater this incident really sits close to my heart I have flown   4. @calvinnickel9995 [1 likes]: Ive got over 5000 hours on the King Air 300 variants.  

The use of VS is appropriate for instances when you need to expedite the climb… like vertical deviations over thunderstorms or if ATC tells you to expedite the climb.  

This is because FLC is very slow.  It was originally designed to change flight levels so it produces very low G forces.. so that flight attendants could continue meal and drink service in the back. Think of it like an elevator… capable of very high speeds.. but it takes a long time to speed up or slow down.  

The important thing when using VS or PITCH to initiate the climb is to:

1) Set a VS or PITCH that the aircraft is capable of maintaining.  

2) Brief the other pilot about the non-standard configuration and the risks.  

3) Add power as appropriate.  

4) Be prepared to engage FLC when airspeed is too low.  

Me personally.. I always keep my finger on the FLC button ready to engage it if I’m climbing in VS.  

One of the perils of automation is that it does a lot of things very well.. but requires CONSTANT monitoring.  Especially in pilot command modes like HDG, PITCH, and VS as well as Altitude Capture mode where you have to anticipate the change in power (classic power off stall is going down on VNAV PATH, capturing selected altitude, and running out of airspeed because your power is still set for descent).

- Gulfstream G650 vs. Bombardier Global 7500 – Jet Battle of the Billionaires: Kind: captions Language: en Two titans of the sky locked in an aerial duel that most of us will only ever witness from economy class. The Gulfream G650 and the Bombardier Global 7,500. These aren't just private jets. They're airborne empires where billiondoll deals close before touchdown. Where the ultra wealthy decide the fate of markets over vintage champagne. And where the line between luxury and absurdity becomes beautifully blurred. Today, we're putting these flying fortunes head-to-head in the ultimate high alitude showdown. The G650, Gulfream's legendary flagship that revolutionized ultra-ongrange travel versus Bombardier's game-changing global 7,500. The jet that dared to redefine what's possible at 41,000 ft. But when two aeronautical heavyweights enter the ring, only one can emerge as the undisputed champion of the stratosphere. So, buckle up. Turbulence not included. Cabin. Let's start with the reason billionaires buy these jets in the first place. The cabin. Think of it as the world's most exclusive real estate with a view that changes by the hour. The Gulfream G650 offers a cabin that stretches 53 feet and 7 in long with a width of 8 ft and 6 in and a height of 6 ft and 5 in. What Gulfream does brilliantly is maximize natural light with 16 of their signature oval windows, the largest in the industry. The G650's cabin bathes in sunshine, creating an atmosphere that feels more like a luxury penthouse than an aircraft. The windows are positioned at exactly the right height, so seated passengers can actually see the horizon without craning their necks. A subtle but meaningful detail that shows GFRA's obsession with passenger comfort. The cabin accommodates up to 18 passengers with handcrafted seats that convert into fully flat beds. Now step aboard the Global 7,500 and you'll immediately notice the difference. This isn't just a cabin. It's a four zone living space stretching 54 feet and 5 in long. Bombardier has created what they call the living suite, dining suite, entertainment suite, and master suite. Essentially giving passengers four distinct rooms at cruising altitude. The master suite features a permanent bed, not a conversion, with a full-size shower in its onsuite bathroom. Yes, you heard that right, a proper shower at 41,000 ft. The Global's newage seats, French for cloud, feature the first new seat architecture in business aviation in 30 years with a deep recline position and a floating base that tracks forward as you recline. Where the G650 exudes classic luxury with its symmetrical design and timeless aesthetics, the Global 7,500 embraces a more residential approach. Both maintain a low cabin altitude of less than 5,500 ft when cruising at 41,000 ft, reducing fatigue. And both keep cabin noise levels whisper quiet under 49 dB. The Global 7,500 pulls decisively ahead with its sole lighting system, dynamic daylight simulation technology that helps reduce jet lag by subtly adjusting cabin lighting to help passengers bodies adapt to destination time zones. The G650 counters with its 100% fresh air replenishment system that replaces all cabin air every 2 minutes. In a post-pandemic world, this air quality advantage resonates strongly with healthconscious executives. Cockpit. Moving to the cockpit, the G650 features Gulfream's Plane View 2 avionic suite with four 14in LCD displays, synthetic vision, and enhanced visual systems that transform the most challenging approaches into a video gamelike experience. The G650's heads up display projects critical flight information onto a transparent screen in the pilot's line of sight, allowing them to monitor instruments while keeping eyes outside the aircraft. Combined with its enhanced vision system, essentially night vision for pilots, the G650 can land in conditions that would ground lesser aircraft. The Global 7,500 counters with its Bombardier Vision flight deck featuring four 15-in displays with the industry's first combined vision system, merging infrared and synthetic imagery for unprecedented situational awareness. What sets the 7,500 apart is its flybywire system with side stick controllers instead of traditional yolks, creating a more spacious cockpit and offering finer control inputs. This advanced flight control system also enables the 7,500's envelope protection features, preventing pilots from exceeding the aircraft's performance limitations. Performance now, now let's talk raw performance, because what good is a luxury suite if it can't get you where you need to go? The G650 is powered by two Rolls-Royce BR725 engines, each producing 16,100 lb of thrust. This gives it a maximum speed of Mach 0.925, about 700 mph, making it among the fastest civilian aircraft in the sky. With a maximum range of 7,500 nautical miles, the G650 can fly non-stop from New York to Beijing or Los Angeles to Sydney. It climbs like a rocket, reaching 41,000 ft in just 20 minutes and cruises comfortably at 51,000 ft, well above commercial traffic and weather. The Global 7,500, meanwhile, is powered by two GE Passport engines, each delivering 18,920 lb of thrust. Where the 7,500 truly shines is range. At 7,700 nautical miles, it can fly 200 nautical miles farther than the G650. The difference between a non-stop flight and an inconvenient fuel stop. The 7,500's superior range comes partly from its advanced wing design. Bombardier's smooth flex wing adapts throughout the flight, optimizing its profile for different phases. During takeoff and landing, it maximizes lift. At cruise, it minimizes drag. Costs. Now for the question everyone wants answered. How much do these flying palaces cost? A new Gulfream G650 will set you back approximately $65 million before customization. Want a particular wood veneer or handstitched leather? Add several million more. Annual operating costs. Expect to spend about $4 million to $5 million if you fly about 400 hours per year. That's roughly $10,000 per hour. If you're just looking to charter, expect to pay between $9,000 and $11,000 per hour depending on the route and demand. The global $7,500 commands and even higher premium. Base price starts at $73 million before your personal touches are added. Operating costs run slightly higher than the G650 at around $5 million to $5.5 million annually or about $12,500 per hour of flight time. Charter rates for the Global 7,500 run between $10,500 and $13,000 per hour. The price of ultimate exclusivity. Beyond the purchase price, ownership involves complex considerations. Both aircraft require crews of at least four, two pilots and two cabin crew with annual salaries exceeding $500,000. Hangar space at prime airports can cost upwards of $250,000 annually. Insurance. Expect to pay about $500,000 per year. And don't forget maintenance. These aircraft require service after every 100 to 200 flight hours with major inspections costing millions. Engine overhauls needed every 3,000 to 4,000 hours can cost 4 million to $5 million per engine. Even among billionaires, outright ownership of these jets is rare. Most opt for fractional ownership programs or jet cards that provide access without the full financial burden of ownership. So, which flying Fortress reigns supreme? The Gulfream G650 offers slightly better airport performance, a legacy of proven reliability, and a cabin bathed in natural light. The Global 7500 counters with greater range, a four zone cabin with dedicated bedroom and advanced anti-jet lag technology. For pure speed and airport flexibility, the G650 holds the edge. For ultimate space and range, the Global 7,500 takes the crown. In this rarified air, the choice often comes down to personal preference rather than objective superiority. B.

- Gulfstream G650 vs Bombardier Global 7500 - Full Review: Kind: captions Language: en Welcome enthusiasts. Today we bring you a head-to-head showdown between two titans of ultra long range private aviation. Picture stepping into the exclusive world where performance, luxury, and capability converge. On one side stands the Gulfream G650, an icon of speed and range admired across the globe for its cuttingedge technology and design. On the other, the Bombaday Global 7500, a modern marvel renowned for its unmatched range and cabin sophistication. Both jets are crafted to carry their passengers across continents in seamless comfort and with unrivaled efficiency. They aren't just jets. They're statements of purpose, power, and prestige. The GFream G650, introduced in 2012, became an instant icon with its sleek aesthetics and groundbreaking range capabilities. In contrast, the Bomberday Global 7500, certified in 2018, aimed to outclass its competition with even more range and cabin versatility. While both jets cater to elite flyers, they embody different philosophies of design and operation. In this detailed comparison, we'll explore each aircraft's acquisition cost, operational expenses, and life cycle. We'll also look into cabin comfort, financing possibilities, and how these jets transform personal and business travel. The race between these two industry leaders is closer than ever. So, buckle up as we dive into this ultimate jet matchup. Acquiring a GFream G650 is no small feat. With prices reflecting its prestige and performance, a pre-owned G650 typically costs around $42 million. While newer models can command upwards of $65 million depending on their configuration, the extended range G650 ER variant can even touch the $70 million mark. Meanwhile, depreciation remains moderate due to the G650's strong resale market. Customization options can affect pricing considerably as owners often select bespoke interiors. Evaluating the investment value is crucial for prospective buyers. Bomberday's Global 7500 being newer and more advanced in several aspects comes with a steeper price tag. A brand new Global 7500 costs approximately $75 million, while used models vary from 61 to $72 million. These prices depend on the jet's age, condition, and level of customization. Thanks to its advanced avionics and superior range, the Global 7500 commands high resale values. Its recent market entry also ensures that most pre-owned units are relatively young. Buyers should anticipate competitive pricing and limited depreciation in the first few years. When comparing these two jets, it's important to note how price correlates with capability. While the G650 often appeals to those valuing brand prestige and proven track record, the Global 7500 attracts owners seeking cuttingedge tech and maximum range. Overall investment in either aircraft includes not only the sticker price, but also considerations like customization, maintenance plans, and potential resale. Market dynamics and demand also influence value retention. Prospective buyers must assess both short-term costs and long-term asset performance. Fixed operating costs are a major aspect of private jet ownership, and the G650 is no exception. Annual fixed expenses, including hanger fees, crew salaries, insurance, and scheduled maintenance, typically total between 1.3 and $ 1.5 million. These figures can vary significantly depending on the aircraft's base of operation and usage patterns. A hanger in a major metropolitan area will cost more than one in a rural region, contributing to variance. Insurance premiums are influenced by the aircraft's mission profile and pilot qualifications. Salaries for a professional crew can easily top six figures per year, $500 to $600,000. All these elements form the backbone of fixed operational budgeting. The Bomberday Global 7500 with its larger footprint and complex systems incur slightly higher fixed costs. Owners typically budget around 1.4 to 1.6 million annually to cover essentials like hanger rental, crew compensation, insurance, and scheduled maintenance. The jet's advanced technology and greater cabin volume can lead to increased servicing requirements. Sophisticated systems demand specialized technicians, which can impact service contracts and costs. Some owners opt for extended manufacturer support programs to ensure thorough coverage. The investment supports greater in-flight luxury and mission flexibility. The added expense may prove worthwhile depending on usage intensity. Comparing the two, the differences in fixed cost isn't dramatic, but accumulates over time, particularly for frequent flyers. Your operational strategy, whether high frequency, long haul travel, or occasional executive missions, will influence which aircraft offers better value. Aircraft management firms can streamline budgeting and optimize cost efficiency. Tax treatments, and depreciation schedules also play into long-term financial planning. Strategic ownership structuring can improve capital recovery. Ultimately, forecasting and proactive budgeting are essential tools. These jets demand planning as elite as their performance. Variable operating costs fluctuate based on how much you fly and under what conditions. For the G650, fuel burn averages about $475 gall per hour, leading to a fuel cost of around $2,850 at $6 per gallon, including maintenance, landing, and handling fees. The G650's cost per flight hour is around $10,000. Engine and parts wear also contribute significantly to hourly expenses and can increase costs if not managed well. Opting into engine maintenance programs can reduce surprise costs and smooth cash flow. High usage amplifies these variable figures quickly, especially for frequent intercontinental trips. Accurate budgeting for each hour flowing is crucial to long-term ownership planning. The Global 7500, while larger, is surprisingly efficient in its class thanks to modern engine design and optimized aerodynamics. It burns approximately $500 to 530 gall of fuel per hour, costing about 3,00 to $3,180 per hour at current fuel rates. Total variable costs land around 10 to $11,000 per flight hour, which includes expenses like maintenance, parts, landing fees, and crew per DM. This efficiency makes it more economical over long hauls despite its size. Maintenance costs are also influenced by usage and the inclusion of support programs which help avoid unexpected expenses. Regular inspections help maintain performance and predict costs more accurately over time. These factors contribute to stable and efficient operations. When considering total variable expenses, both aircraft are in the same ballpark with marginal differences that could sway a costconscious owner. Choosing between them may come down to specific operational needs like average flight time, destinations, and airport access. If you frequently fly long transcontinental or trans oceanionic routes, the Global 7500's extra range can offer savings by avoiding repositioning or fuel stops. Conversely, the G650's widespread service network and proven reliability may appeal for more highfrequency global travelers. Understanding your typical mission profile is essential when evaluating operational costs. A detailed cost analysis based on real travel patterns is recommended. The GFream G650 is a powerhouse of performance, offering a maximum range of 7,000 nautical miles. It flies at a top speed of Mach 0.925 and high-speed cruises comfortably at Mach 0.90. With a maximum service ceiling of 51,000 ft, it can soar above weather and commercial traffic. These features make it ideal for non-stop flights between major international cities. Flight planning is straightforward, and the aircraft can utilize shorter runways than many jets in its class. This performance mix allows for both speed and flexibility. The Bombberday Global 7500 takes it a step further, boasting a range of 7,700 nautical miles. Like the G650, it reaches a top speed of Mach 0.925, but adds more endurance to the mix. Its service ceiling also hits 51,000 ft, ensuring a smooth ride above turbulence. The Global 7500 can link virtually any two cities in the world without refueling. This extended capability allows for more direct routing and fewer stops. It's particularly suited for highfrequency, long ultra range missions. Both jets are well suited for the global traveler with performance metrics that exceed most business needs. While the G650 offers slightly better runway flexibility, the 7500 shines with its extended range and newer systems. Factors like flight duration, typical routes, and airport access should guide your decision. Both aircraft are capable of impressive performance under diverse conditions. Your mission profile ultimately determines the better fit. Cabin comfort is a highlight of the Gulfream G650, which seats up to 19 passengers across multiple zones. The cabin stretches 46 ft 10 in in length and spans 8'2 in in width, offering plenty of room for relaxation, work, and dining. With its signature panoramic windows, the interior is bathed in natural light, enhancing the spacious feel. A low cabin altitude reduces fatigue during long flights, and the noise insulation ensures a serene experience. Entertainment systems, high-speed connectivity, and custom lighting controls round out the luxury. Each G650 is tailored to the owner's preferences, offering a unique blend of style and function. The Global 7500 takes luxury to new heights with its impressive four zone cabin layout that measures over 54 ft, 5 in in length. Inside you'll find a full galley capable of preparing gourmet meals, a permanent bedroom for restful sleep, and the only stand-up shower in its class. The Global 7500 can accommodate up to 19 passengers. The newage seating system, exclusive to Bomber Day, provides unmatched ergonomic comfort on lawn halls. Mood lighting, touchscreen environmental controls, and high-speed internet enhance the passengers experience. Whether for work or leisure, the cabin supports a seamless journey. It's effectively a luxury penthouse at 51,000 ft. Both cabins set the standard for in-flight comfort and functionality, making them favorites among global travelers, while the G650 offers exceptional ambiance and customizability. The Global 7500 adds more physical space and built-in luxury. Mission specific needs often determine the better choice between them. Some prioritize a dedicated bedroom. Others prefer customizable workspaces. For non-stop global routes, the 7500's amenities are particularly appealing. Yet, Gfream's meticulous design philosophy continues to impress even the most discerning jet owners. In the end, both cabins provide elite high performance luxury above the clouds. Owning either of these jets opens up a lifestyle of freedom and efficiency. They're ideal for corporate executives managing international operations, enabling direct travel between headquarters and global branches. Celebrities and high-n networth individuals also benefit from the privacy and convenience they offer. They allow access to destinations not served by commercial airlines on your schedule. Families can use these aircraft for leisure, flying to remote or exclusive vacation spots with minimal hassle. These jets can be configured for personal use with sleeping areas, dining setups, and entertainment systems. They support a seamless travel experience, especially for those who value discretion. Jet ownership also enhances travel readiness for emergencies or lastminute decisions. Convenience becomes part of daily life. Additionally, owners may choose to offset costs through chartering. Management companies can handle bookings, crew staffing, and maintenance. While this approach won't make ownership profitable, it can defay some operational expenses. Strategic planning and usage optimization are key. Jet cards and fractional programs are alternative pathways for those not ready for full ownership. Financing such a significant purchase involves multiple options. Banks, leasing firms, and specialized aviation lenders provide structured solutions. Loan terms typically range from 5 to 15 years with competitive interest rates. Some buyers opt for leasing to preserve capital for other ventures. Tax implications and asset depreciation are important considerations. Consulting a financial adviser is essential. The life cycle of a private jet like the G650 or the Global 7500 can span 30 years or more. With proper care, these aircraft retain excellent operational and resale value. Routine inspections, component upgrades, and refurbishments are part of the journey. Manufacturer support programs extend service life and ensure compliance. Technology updates also improve avionics and performance. These jets age gracefully with proper stewardship. Planning for resale is also a wise strategy. Gulfream and Bombberday aircraft typically maintain strong market demand. Documentation, flight logs, and maintenance history significantly impact resale value. Choosing popular configurations can enhance appeal to future buyers. Ownership is as much about the journey as it is the investment. Strategic planning ensures long-term satisfaction. In conclusion, the Gulfream G650 and the Barbad Global 7500 are elite aircraft that redefine what's possible in private aviation. The G650 delivers reliability, brand prestige, and proven performance. While the Global 7500 brings innovation, extended range, and unparalleled luxury. Ownership involves more than just the purchase. It's a commitment to a lifestyle. With proper planning and management, these jets offer unmatched mobility, comfort, and value. For global achievers and discerning travelers, either choice is a gateway to the world. Choose wisely and the sky is truly the limit.
