- The $75M Showdown: Gulfstream G650 vs Bombardier Global 7500: Kind: captions Language: en One of these jets can fly you from New York to Hong Kong nonstop while you sleep in a literal master suite. The other has been the undisputed king of the skies for a decade, favored by the world's most elite CEOs. But today, the crown is slipping. Behind the $75 million price tags lies a secret. One of these aircraft has a hidden engineering flaw that the other solved with a revolutionary wing design. By the end of this video, you'll see exactly why one billionaire just spent $5 million just to cancel his order for one and switch to the other. Welcome to the ultimate showdown. The Gulfream G650 versus the Bombardier Global 7500. In the world of ultra-ong range private aviation, there are planes and then there are time machines. For years, the Gulfream G650 was the gold standard. If you were a member of the Forbes 400, you owned a G650. It was the ultimate status symbol, combining Mach.925 speed with the iconic oval windows that told the world you had arrived. But then, Bombardier did the unthinkable. They didn't just build a competitor. They built a clean sheet disruptor. The Global 7500 arrived with more range, more rooms, and a seating technology that claims to cure jet lag using NASA inspired science. Today, we aren't just looking at leather seats in goldplated cup holders. We are diving deep into the avionics, the thrust to weight ratios, the cabin altitudes, and the brutal depreciation cycles to find out which jet truly earns the title of best in the world. Let's start with the first thing anyone sees, the ramp presence, the Gulfream G650. When you pull up to a private terminal in a G650, people know. Its 16 massive oval windows are the largest in aviation. It's a design language that screams old money. But don't let the classic looks fool you. The G650 is a beast. With a wingspan of nearly 100 ft and those signature swept back wings, it's designed to slice through the air at 92% the speed of sound. The Global 7500. The Global 7500 is longer, sleeker, and looks like a Predator. But its real secret isn't the length. It's the smooth flex wing. Unlike the rigid wings on older jets, the 7500's wing acts like a shock absorber for the air. It flexes and bends to neutralize turbulence. While G650 passengers might feel a bump at 45,000 ft, Global 7500 passengers are often told they've landed because they didn't even realize they were descending. Let's look at the nerd numbers. This is where the 30,000 mph deals are made. Range. The standard G650 offers 7,000 nautical miles. The G650 ER extended range bumps that to 7,500 nautical miles. That's London to Buenoises without breaking a sweat. But the Global 7500, it holds the record for the longest flight ever by a purpose-built business jet. It boasts a staggering 7700 nautical mile range. That extra 200 miles might not sound like much, but it's the difference between landing in Singapore or having to stop for fuel in a place you'd rather not be. It connects city pairs like New York to Hong Kong or San Francisco to Singapore with ease. Speed. Both jets are capped at a maximum operating speed of Mach 0.925. At this speed, you're flying just below the sound barrier. However, there is a nuance here. The G650 was built for high-speed cruise efficiency. It wants to go fast. The Global 7500, powered by the massive GE Passport engines, provide nearly 19,000 lbs of thrust per side. These aren't just engines. They are scaled down versions of the engines found on the Boeing 787 Dreamliner. They're quieter, more efficient, and reach their top speed with less vibration. Here is where the Global 7500 starts to pull ahead in the engineering race. Usually, the bigger the jet, the longer the runway it needs. The G650 requires about 5,858 ft of runway to get off the ground at maximum weight. That's standard for this class. But the Global 7500, despite being a larger, heavier aircraft, can take off in just 5,800 ft. Why does this matter? It means the Global 7500 can access tight airports like London City or Aspen, Colorado, where other heavy jets are banned. Imagine owning a $75 million jet and being told you can't land at your favorite ski resort. With the Global, that's rarely an issue. If the exterior is about status, the interior is about survival. When you're flying 14 hours straight, the cabin isn't just a room, it's your entire world. The Gulfream G650 is legendary for its spaciousness. It offers a cabin width of 8'6 in, which is slightly wider than the Bombardier. This extra width makes the aisle feel more like a hallway in a luxury home. Typically, the G650 is divided into three distinct living zones. You have your forward galley and crew rest, a main meeting area, and a rear lounge that can be cordoned off for privacy. It's clean, it's symmetrical, and it's bathed in light from those 16 panoramic windows. But here is where the Bombardier Global 7500 strikes a heavy blow. It is the only purpose-built business jet in the world with four true living zones. Because the Fuselage is over 11 ft longer than the Gulfream, Bombardier didn't just add more seats, they added more rooms. In a Global 7500, you don't just have a rear lounge. You have a principal suite. We're talking about a permanent bedroom with a real bed and an optional onsuite master shower. In the Gulfream, your bed is usually a de van or seats that have been folded down by the flight attendant. In the Global, you walk into a bedroom that looks like a high-end boutique hotel. For the CEO who needs to go straight from the runway to a board meeting, that shower and a real night's sleep are worth the $75 million price tag alone. Let's talk about where you'll spend 90% of your time. The seat. GFream seats are hand-crafted masterpieces of leather and foam. They're comfortable, iconic, and offer excellent lumbar support. But Bombardier decided that comfortable wasn't enough. They spent seven years developing the Noage seat. The Noage is the first new seat architecture in business aviation in over 30 years. It features a tilt link system that allows for a zeroravity position. Unlike traditional seats that just lean back and put pressure on your lower back, the Noage tilts the entire base of the seat, shifting your weight to simulate weightlessness. It's designed to improve circulation and reduce the physical toll that longhaul flying takes on the body. When you sit in a newage, you aren't just sitting, you're floating. It's a level of ergonomic engineering that Gulfream, as great as their seats are, simply hasn't matched yet. Have you ever wondered why you feel exhausted after a long flight? It's not just the time change, it's the air. Most commercial planes are pressurized to 8,000 ft. At that altitude, your blood oxygen levels drop and your heart works harder. The Gulfream G650 fought back by offering a low cabin altitude. At a cruising height of 41,000 ft, the G650 maintains a cabin altitude of just 3,290 ft. That is industry-leading. It means your body feels like it's standing on a small hill rather than a mountain. However, the Global 7500 has pushed the boundary even further. It offers a cabin altitude of 2,900 ft while flying at 41,000 ft. It also features the Pooh Air system, which uses a hospital-grade HPA filter to replace the entire cabin every 90 seconds. While the G650 Air is incredibly fresh, the 7500's combination of the lowest altitude and the fastest air replacement makes it arguably the healthiest environment in the sky. Then there is the Sole lighting system. Gulfream uses a very high-end circadian lighting system that changes color temperature based on the time of day. It's beautiful and effective. But Bombardier took it a step further by integrating the lighting directly into the flight management system. The CET system doesn't just change colors. It uses specific wavelengths of red and blue light to actively stimulate or suppress melatonin. If you are flying from London to Tokyo, the plane knows exactly when to wake up your brain and when to prepare it for sleep based on your destination's time zone. It's like having a digital doctor managing your internal clock. When you combine this with the Global's 28 windows, which Bombardier added specifically to rival Gulfream's light-filled reputation, the result is a cabin that feels alive. Most people ignore the kitchen or the galley until they're hungry over the Atlantic. The G650 has a worldclass galley, but the Global 7500 features the largest galley in business aviation. It's designed by professional chefs. It features a dual function convection microwave oven that can actually fresh cook a meal rather than just reheating it. It also has an advanced chiller that reaches its cooling temperature in just 11 minutes. Compared to the 90 minutes it takes on almost every other jet. If you want a five-star dining experience at Mach 0.9, the Global is currently the kitchen king. While the passengers are sleeping in zeroravity seats, two people are working at Mach 0.9 to keep them safe. And the way they fly these two jets couldn't be more different. The Gulfream G650 features the Symmetry Flight Deck. It was a revolution in aviation because it replaced the traditional floormounted control yolks with active control side sticks. If you've ever played a high-end flight simulator, you'll recognize the look. But these aren't just joysticks. They are active, meaning if the pilot on the left moves their stick, the pilot on the right feels that same movement in their own hand. This creates a tactile link between the crew that you don't get in other side stick controlled aircraft like Airbuses. It's sleek, it's intuitive, and it uses 10 touchscreen controllers to declutter the cockpit. It looks like the bridge of a starship. The Bombardier Global 7,500 takes a different approach with its vision flight deck. Based on the Rockwell Collins Proline Fusion Suite, it features four massive 15-in displays. While Gulfream went for the futuristic side stick, Bombadier stuck with a more traditional yoke layout, but packed it with the most advanced flybywire system in the industry. The Global's computer system is so advanced, it can automatically compensate for wind shear and turbulence before the pilot even feels it. Which is better? Pilots are divided. Those who love tech in the future swear by the Gulfream side sticks. Those who want a rugged, familiar feel with the world's most advanced digital safety net choose the Bombader. But both jets share one ultimate safety feature, enhanced vision systems, EVS. They can land in total fog or darkness using infrared cameras that project a clear image of the runway onto a glass screen in front of the pilot's eyes. In these jets, weather is rarely an excuse for being late. Now, let's get into the numbers that make even billionaires sweat. Owning one of these is like owning a small country. To fly a Gulfream G650 for 200 hours a year, you are looking at an annual budget of roughly $6.5 million. Fuel alone will cost you about $3,140 per hour. Engine programs and maintenance add another $2,000 per hour. Then you have the fixed costs. Crew salaries, hanger fees, and expensive cities like New York or London. And insurance will run you about $1.1 million a year before you even start the engines. The Global 7500 is slightly more expensive to operate, largely because it is a bigger, heavier machine with more powerful GE Passport engines. While the fuel burn is incredibly efficient for its size, you're still moving a four zone flying apartment. Variable costs for the Global can push towards $4,000 to $4,500 per hour depending on the price of Jet A fuel. But here is the hidden cost no one tells you about, the crew. Because these jets fly missions lasting 14 plus hours, you can't just have two pilots. You need a third pilot to rotate in for rest and often a highly trained flight attendant who specializes in silver service dining. You aren't just paying for a plane. You're paying for a 24/7 global flight department. If you want to know which jet is better for your wallet, you have to look at the resale market. A brand new Global 7500 lists for about 73 million to $75 million. A brand new GFream G650 ER is slightly less around $70 million to $72 million. However, GFream has a secret weapon, brand loyalty. Gulfreams tend to hold their value like Ferraris. Because there is such a massive global network of Gulfream odors. Finding a buyer for a used G650 is relatively easy. You can find a 10-year-old G650 today still selling for 35 million to $45 million. So, which jet is better? It depends on your mission profile. Choose the Gulfream G650 ER if you value brand prestige, the best resale value in history, and that iconic light-filled oval window aesthetic. It is the Rolex of the sky. Everyone recognizes it, and it never goes out of style. Choose the Global 7500 if you actually live on your plane. If you need a permanent bedroom to arrive in Tokyo ready for a10 billion dollar negotiation, the four zone layout and the newage zero gravity seats are mathematically superior for your health and productivity.

The physical "TOGA" button shown in this tutorial has been changed to an "Autothrottle Disconnect" button (which is realistic to the real plane). There is now an invisible TOGA click-spot slightly forward (toward the nose of the plane) of that thumb button.

development marred by financial problems and Technical challenges the sf-50 was certified by the FAA on October 28 2016. with certification the Cirrus Vision sf50 became the first civilian single-engine jet to be type certified with 15 percent of all orders destined to be sent to Europe Cirrus received its eaasa in May 2017. do you know that from its Inception till a prototype was offers seating for up to five adults including the pilot and two children spread across three rows all seats are forward-facing so there's no playing footsie with other passengers the seats in the second row have ample legroom and a generous recline all seats offer easy access to built-in USB ports power outlets cup holders overhead lights and air vents similar to an SUV there's no bathroom or Galley on board however there is a 22-inch entertainment screen that drops down from the ceiling it can be used for working or watching movies but abgeeks will likely prefer to entertain themselves with the massive Windows the windows don't have Shades but are UV tinted for flexibility five rear seats can be removed easily by the pilot and the new layout can be configured in the sf50s avionics for Weight and Balance purposes more importantly the plane offers a slew of unique safety features with a safe return system the plane can land itself at the nearest airport with the Press of a button if things go really wrong there's also a parachute designed to Bear the weight of the entire aircraft that can be deployed by pulling a red lever on the ceiling other features include an infrared camera for increased awareness when flying at night and in bad weather well the Williams f-500 and 35 5A engine produces 1800 on thrust and allows the vision jet to cruise at 345 miles per hour the plane has a maximum range of 1 275 nautical miles but averages around 950 miles that's enough to fly between City pairs like New York and Chicago Boston and Atlanta and Phoenix and Dallas it cruises around 350 miles per hour and has a maximum altitude of 31 000 feet allowing it to Fly Above bad weather these specs are slightly below the Honda Jet but that plane costs roughly twice as much once in the air the aircraft is very friendly to hand fly as all kinds of AIDS to make the experience Pleasant the aircraft also has a yaw stability system that automatically kicks in at 200 feet the engine Private jets like never before. When it comes to creating a private jet, everything starts with a dream. A vision of luxury, speed, and comfort soaring through the clouds. In this section, we'll take you deep into the heart of the design process where imagination takes flight. Private jet design is a delicate dance between artistry and functionality. A team of skilled designers, architects, and engineers come together to brainstorm, sketch, and refine the aircraft's concept. Their goal is to craft an aerodynamic masterpiece that not only turns heads, but also maximizes performance. The exterior design is where the aircraft's personality takes shape. Every curve, line, and contour is meticulously evaluated to ensure both elegance and efficiency. Computer aided design CAD software allows designers to visualize the aircraft from all angles, ensuring every aspect is harmonious and visually striking. Step by step, the vision transitions from paper to a three-dimensional model, a miniature representation of the future private jet. This model allows the team to see how the aircraft will look in real life and make any necessary adjustments before moving on to the next phase. Meanwhile, the interior design takes on an equally vital role. Here, the focus is on creating an oasis of comfort and opulence for the jet's elite passengers. The design team works closely with clients to tailor every inch of the interior to their preferences, crafting a space that feels like a luxurious extension of their homes or offices. Materials play a crucial role in turning these concepts into reality. From lavish leathers and exotic woods to state-of-the-art composite materials, every choice is made with both aesthetics and functionality in mind. The goal is to strike the perfect balance between luxury and practicality, ensuring the aircraft is not only visually stunning, but also durable and efficient. Once the final design is approved, it's time to bring the dream to life. The chosen design is meticulously translated into engineering drawings ready to guide the manufacturing process. Every detail down to the placement of each light fixture and the stitching on the seats is documented to ensure accuracy during construction. In this intricate world of design, every stroke of the pen and every click of the mouse paves the way for the creation of a private jet that is not only a marvel of engineering, but also a true work of art. A testament to human ingenuity and the pursuit of excellence in aviation. With the design blueprint in hand, the next crucial step in crafting a private jet is selecting the finest materials and employing precision engineering to build a flying masterpiece. The materials chosen must meet the highest standards of quality, durability, and safety. Advanced composite materials like carbon fiber and titanium alloys are often preferred for their exceptional strengthtoe ratio, ensuring the aircraft remains both lightweight and robust. These cuttingedge materials not only contribute to improved fuel efficiency, but also enhance the aircraft's overall performance and longevity. Meticulous precision is the cornerstone of private jet manufacturing. The construction process involves an intricate series of steps where skilled technicians and engineers work in perfect harmony to piece together each component of the aircraft. From the fuselage to the wings, every part is meticulously crafted, measured, and fitted with utmost care. Computer numerical control CNC machines play a vital role in achieving this level of precision. These high-tech devices use computer programming to precisely shape and cut the materials, ensuring a seamless and exact fit during assembly. The smallest discrepancy can have a significant impact on the aircraft's performance, making precision engineering an absolute necessity. Throughout the manufacturing process, rigorous quality control checks are conducted at every stage. These include non-destructive testing, stress analysis, and thorough inspections to guarantee the structural integrity and safety of the aircraft. As the various components come together, it becomes evident that the aircraft is more than just a sum of its parts. It is a harmonious fusion of advanced materials and precision engineering. A flying work of art that represents the pinnacle of human achievement in aviation. The assembly process is a symphony of collaboration and precision with each member of the skilled team playing their part. It all begins with the fuselage, the aircraft's backbone. Using cuttingedge manufacturing techniques, the fuselage is meticulously formed and joined together, creating the foundation for the rest of the aircraft. Next, the wings gracefully come into position. These vital components are carefully integrated, their aerodynamic design contributing to the jet's effortless flight. The team ensures that every wing is aligned precisely, guaranteeing optimal stability and lift during takeoff and landing. As the assembly line progresses, the aircraft's interior starts to come alive. Lavish furnishings, state-of-the-art avionics, and luxurious amenities are meticulously installed. Technicians pay unwavering attention to every detail, ensuring that the interior is a harmonious blend of sophistication and comfort. Wiring and systems integration are crucial to the jet's functionality. A network of cables and advanced electronic systems is skillfully woven throughout the aircraft. This intricate web connects everything from lighting and entertainment to navigation and safety systems, creating a seamless experience for its passengers. Finally, the engines are positioned with utmost care, the heart of the aircraft that will power it to new heights. Skilled technicians conduct rigorous testing to ensure the engines are functioning flawlessly and delivering the required performance. Before a private jet is cleared for takeoff, it underos an exhaustive fine-tuning process to ensure it operates with impeccable precision. From wind tunnel tests to comprehensive safety evaluations, this phase leaves no room for compromise. Engineers analyze every aspect of the jet's performance, making adjustments to optimize aerodynamics, fuel efficiency, and handling. The aircraft is put through its paces, simulating various flight conditions to guarantee it can handle any challenge with grace and ease. Only when the jet passes every test with flying colors is it deemed ready to take to the skies, ushering in a new era of luxury air travel. In the world of private jets, no two aircraft are alike. And this is where the magic of customization and luxury truly shines. Clients with discerning tastes are presented with a wide array of options to tailor their flying experience to perfection. From the moment a client steps into the design studio, their desires and preferences become the focal point. Interior designers collaborate closely, curating a bespoke environment that reflects the client's unique style. Lavish fabrics, sumptuous leathers, and exquisite finishes come together to create an ambiance of opulence and comfort. Cuttingedge technology is seamlessly integrated with state-of-the-art entertainment systems, business amenities, and advanced controls at the client's fingertips. Every detail, from personalized lighting schemes to bespoke cabinetry, is crafted to elevate the private jet to the pinnacle of luxury. This level of customization ensures that each flight becomes a singular experience tailored exclusively to the client's tastes and requirements. A testament to the unwavering commitment of private jet manufacturers to provide the ultimate in opulence and personalized travel. As the final touches are placed and the last inspections are completed, the private jet stands ready for its grand moment, takeoff into the boundless sky. The culmination of innovation, precision, and luxury. The aircraft now exudes an aura of sophistication and grace. The anticipation builds as the flight crew prepares for departure, checking every system with meticulous attention. The engines hum to life, their powerful turbines exuding confidence. Passengers step aboard, greeted by the epitome of comfort and opulence. With a gentle roar, the private jet taxis toward the runway, awaiting clearance for liftoff. As it ascends, a new chapter in luxurious air travel begins, carrying dreams and ambitions high above the clouds. And there you have it, the captivating journey into the world of how it's made. Private jets. From the initial spark of imagination to the meticulous precision of assembly, we've witnessed the birth of a soaring masterpiece. The combination of cuttingedge materials, unrivaled craftsmanship, and personalized luxury showcases the true artistry of private jet manufacturing. As these flying marvels take to the skies, they carry with them the dreams and aspirations of those who seek the pinnacle of comfort and speed. We hope this behindthe-scenes adventure has given you a newfound appreciation for the remarkable ingenuity that fuels the world of private aviation. Until next time, safe travels and blue skies ahead.

- Tour of NOAA King Air aircraft: Kind: captions Language: en Good afternoon everyone, my name is Lieutenant&nbsp; Rick DeTriquet with the NOAA Commissioned&nbsp;&nbsp; Officer Corps. Now we can run&nbsp; them simultaneously, or one at a time. As you can see we we've done that&nbsp;&nbsp; uh several different hurricanes and also with&nbsp; the most recent tornadoes in Nashville as well.&nbsp;&nbsp; but we'll talk a little bit more about the&nbsp; mission. Typical flight profiles for us are&nbsp;&nbsp; 1500 feet to 10,500 feet depending on the&nbsp; mission. Whether it's coastal mapping,&nbsp;&nbsp; normal coastal erosion assessment, we'll typically&nbsp; fly up higher better image quality we can the&nbsp;&nbsp; higher we go we can get a little bit better image&nbsp; quality. But if it's an emergency response mission&nbsp;&nbsp; we'll fly as low as 1500 feet just to get below&nbsp; the clouds and be able to get those clear photos.&nbsp;&nbsp; In those cases it's essential to fly as slow&nbsp; as we can. Our typical survey speeds are 170&nbsp;&nbsp; knots is the sweet spot, but as we get into&nbsp; those lower altitudes we'll try to slow down&nbsp;&nbsp; as much as possible. and our minimum indicated&nbsp; speeds are typically 135 knots at that altitude.&nbsp;&nbsp; Otherwise we can pick a survey altitude in between&nbsp; those and the sweet spot again is going to be&nbsp;&nbsp; 170 knots. We can capture different ports of&nbsp; interest, different neighborhoods of interest,&nbsp;&nbsp; different coastlines, and we can really&nbsp; zoom or focus in on areas of damage.&nbsp;&nbsp; Our primary emergency response mission is to&nbsp; be the first responders after a storm comes by&nbsp;&nbsp; and fly straight to the area of max damage and&nbsp; survey as many many passes as we can. Weather&nbsp;&nbsp; limiting, fuel limiting, traffic limiting, we'll&nbsp; capture photos for five six sometimes seven hours.&nbsp;&nbsp; And as soon as we land the survey operator is&nbsp; passing that that on to a ground support personnel&nbsp;&nbsp; and that data those photos are passed up to the&nbsp; RSD - remote sensing division - where it can be&nbsp;&nbsp; utilized by all of our different stakeholders&nbsp; for emergency response on scene or further on&nbsp;&nbsp; support work down the road as as that damage of&nbsp; the hurricane progresses. You can see our two&nbsp;&nbsp; cameras here they're both the Applanix cameras&nbsp; and they are our typical outfit for the mission. I've been in NOAA for eight years with&nbsp; the NOAA Commissioned Officer Corps.&nbsp;&nbsp; I went to a ship for two years, the NOAA&nbsp; Ship Nancy Foster, and I had a blast doing&nbsp;&nbsp; all sorts of different dive ops, small boat&nbsp; ops, survey ops. Thanks everyone&nbsp;&nbsp; for joining us on the tour of the NOAA King Air,&nbsp; hope you guys enjoy the rest of the tour of NOAA. Hi, I'm Lieutenant Coleen Conley from&nbsp; Ellicot City, Maryland. I commissioned&nbsp;&nbsp; out of the Naval Academy in 2009 with a degree in&nbsp; oceanography. I flew the P-3 Orion for the Navy&nbsp;&nbsp; for 10 years and transitioned to the NOAA Corps&nbsp; in October 2019. Here at NOAA I'm an aviation&nbsp;&nbsp; safety officer and I fly the Beechcraft King&nbsp; Air. Come on back, let's have a look inside So here we are in the back of the King&nbsp; Air. The first thing you're going to notice&nbsp;&nbsp; are these two big cameras right here. We have both&nbsp; an oblique and a nadir camera, which allows us to&nbsp;&nbsp; perform our primary mission sets. We take both&nbsp; pre- and post-event imagery. Pre-event imagery&nbsp;&nbsp; occurs prior to hurricane season every year. We&nbsp; fly the East Coast, the Gulf Coast, and around&nbsp;&nbsp; the U.S. territories in the Caribbean and collect&nbsp; imagery of the coastline. This imagery will serve&nbsp;&nbsp; as a baseline, or standard for comparison, in&nbsp; case a hurricane or tropical storm rolls through&nbsp;&nbsp; and then our post-event imagery, also known as&nbsp; emergency response imagery, will be collected&nbsp;&nbsp; and then we can make comparisons to see what's&nbsp; changed. This imagery is all publicly available&nbsp;&nbsp; online. That about covers our primary&nbsp; mission sets, we can head up to the cockpit.&nbsp; Welcome to the cockpit. We typically fly these&nbsp; missions with three people on board. We have&nbsp;&nbsp; a pilot in either seat up front and a sensor&nbsp; operator who's controlling the camera in the back.&nbsp;&nbsp; Factors that affect this mission are sun angle&nbsp; and tide windows. The tide windows are important&nbsp;&nbsp; because how high the water is will affect the&nbsp; imagery that we're getting along the coastline.&nbsp;&nbsp; And sun angle is important because you don't&nbsp; want long shadows cast when you're trying to&nbsp;&nbsp; collect imagery of the coastline. The left-hand&nbsp; pilot is flying off of an ipad that's showing&nbsp;&nbsp; them where to fly. So crew resource management is&nbsp; especially important here, because the right side&nbsp;&nbsp; pilot is going to be monitoring your airspace,&nbsp; your traffic, making all the radio calls,&nbsp;&nbsp; setting the nav aids that sort of thing. So you&nbsp; want to make sure everyone's working in unison to&nbsp;&nbsp; keep the aircraft safe at all times. We typically&nbsp; fly around 5500 feet, lower if needed. We can't&nbsp;&nbsp; have any clouds below us which is a pretty&nbsp; stringent requirement but makes sense because&nbsp;&nbsp; the clouds will show up in all of the imagery so&nbsp; that's often a limiting factor for us. but that's&nbsp;&nbsp; the basic gist of how we fly our coastal mapping&nbsp; missions. Thanks for joining me today for a tour&nbsp;&nbsp; of NOAA's Beechcraft King Air enjoy the rest&nbsp; of your tour of the Aircraft Operations Center This is the NOAA King Air. It&nbsp; collects pre- and post-storm imagery&nbsp;&nbsp; like this from Mexico Beach, Florida&nbsp; before and after Hurricane Michael. The United States has 95,000 miles of shoreline.&nbsp; These dynamic coastal regions are vulnerable to&nbsp;&nbsp; flooding, property destruction, and beach erosion&nbsp; from severe weather and sea level change. Coastal&nbsp;&nbsp; areas rely on accurate elevation data to plan&nbsp; and recover when hazardous situations arise.&nbsp;&nbsp; These communities depend on up-to-date information&nbsp; for their management and resiliency planning.&nbsp;&nbsp; As part of its mission, NOAA's National Geodetic&nbsp; Survey (NGS) provides an accurate consistent&nbsp;&nbsp; and current national shoreline. NGS's mean&nbsp; high water line is considered the nation's&nbsp;&nbsp; official shoreline. It is used to create and&nbsp; maintain NOAA nautical charts. These charts&nbsp;&nbsp; are used by commercial and recreational boaters to&nbsp; navigate safely and avoid potential hazards. NGS's&nbsp;&nbsp; shoreline is also a crucial source for defining&nbsp; our nation's boundaries and territorial limits. Remotely-sensed data from aircraft&nbsp; is collected to map the shoreline.&nbsp;&nbsp; The use of aircraft-based sensors, such as&nbsp; a digital camera and topobathy lidar system,&nbsp;&nbsp; allows large areas to be surveyed&nbsp; in a short amount of time.&nbsp;&nbsp; Lidar, which stands for light detection and&nbsp; ranging, is a remote-sensing method that uses a&nbsp;&nbsp; laser to measure distances to a surface. NGS uses&nbsp; a unique type of sensor called topobathy lidar.&nbsp;&nbsp; It collects wide swaths of elevation data on both&nbsp; land, or topography, and in shallow water areas,&nbsp;&nbsp; or bathymetry. Bathymetric data has historically&nbsp; been difficult to acquire in these highly&nbsp;&nbsp; dynamic coastal zones. Since NOAA survey ships&nbsp; cannot travel safely close to the shoreline,&nbsp;&nbsp; NGS's topobathy lidar system uses a green laser&nbsp; that operates in a circular scan pattern to&nbsp;&nbsp; penetrate shallow water near the shore. The high&nbsp; density point data is combined with GPS and other&nbsp;&nbsp; positional data to create precise 3d topographic&nbsp; and bathymetric elevation models. NGS uses&nbsp;&nbsp; coastal elevation data to map the mean high water&nbsp; shoreline, which is a tidally referenced boundary.&nbsp;&nbsp; The shoreline is verified using aerial imagery&nbsp; that is collected along with the lidar data.&nbsp;&nbsp; Topobathy lidar data collected by NGS is&nbsp; freely available from NOAA's Digital Coast&nbsp;&nbsp; to use for applications and tools to&nbsp; support decision making in the coastal zone.&nbsp;&nbsp; Academic and research organizations use&nbsp; coastal elevation lidar data to develop&nbsp;&nbsp; ocean and coastal models. These 3d models&nbsp; help researchers understand ocean circulation.&nbsp;&nbsp; Elevation data is an important part of the model&nbsp; because circulation is influenced by friction,&nbsp;&nbsp; which is related to the height of the sea floor.&nbsp; Coastal engineers and scientists use elevation&nbsp;&nbsp; data to understand sediment movement, beach&nbsp; erosion, and sediment budgets. This information&nbsp;&nbsp; is used to develop management plans to restore,&nbsp; monitor, and maintain beaches in the coastal zone.&nbsp;&nbsp; Elevation data is also one of the most&nbsp; important parameters for modeling inundation.&nbsp;&nbsp; Topobathy lidar data is especially valuable&nbsp; due to its accuracy and its seamless transition&nbsp;&nbsp; from land to water. Coastal managers and&nbsp; decision makers use visualization tools&nbsp;&nbsp; that incorporate these elevation models to&nbsp; understand vulnerability, increase resilience,&nbsp;&nbsp; and develop hazard mitigation plans. These are&nbsp; only a few of the many ways that elevation and&nbsp;&nbsp; shoreline data are being used to help keep&nbsp; coastal communities safe and resilient. NOAA&nbsp;&nbsp; topobathy lidar data is a critical component for&nbsp; meeting the challenges of an uncertain future.

- INCREDIBLE LUXURY PRIVATE JET: Dassault Falcon 8X!: Kind: captions Language: en-US Dasalt Falcon 8X, the quiet power of distance. Visually, you notice Dasalt's signature S-duct feeding the center engine, the long, elegant wing with high-lift sophistication baked into its plan form, and a fuselage stretched just enough to unlock a more flexible cabin without tipping into excess. Under the skin, the jet is all systems thinking. A digital flight control core descended from frontline fighters, a triple redundant power plant philosophy that gives you uncommon flexibility on long oceanic legs and aerodynamics shaped to go far on less fuel. You feel those choices, not as numbers on a spec sheet, but as an attitude, confident, composed, and quietly efficient. Range, speed, and the way. The 8X uses both. Its maximum operating Mach number is 0.90 comma, which means if the jet needs to hustle, it can, but Dassault tunes the airplane for something subtler. Fast enough to matter, gentle enough that passengers arrive lucid. That balance is important in the ultra-long-range category. You can shave a few minutes with raw speed. Wide enough that aisle conversations don't feel like apologizing and long enough, over 42 feet, to carve the space into three distinct living zones without pinching any of them. Designers exploit that length in clever ways. Up front, a club setting, doubles as a working lounge with in-seat power. Side-leged touch control of lighting, temperature and shade, and streaming-ready connectivity that feels like moving a downtown office into a pressure-sealed world of quiet. Because this is a Falcon, the air you breathe has been engineered with the same care as the aerodynamics. Humidity is boosted compared to typical jets. The filtration keeps particulates exceptionally low, and the pressurization system maintains a cabin altitude around the 3900-foot mark, even when you're cruising in the low 40s. Translate that to how you feel. Fewer pressure change headaches, less eye and skin dryness, and considerably more mental clarity as the hours stack up. Whisper-level acoustics are part of it too. Dissolve targets noise at the source, and then layers in insulation where the ear is most sensitive, so conversations stay natural and sleep feels like sleep, not a compromise. A galley that works. A shower that changes everything. Long-range jets are judged harshly on how well they feed you six hours after takeoff. Oven, chiller, real counter space, proper storage, so plated dining looks and tastes like something you'd actually post about. Aft, the lavatory can be optioned with a full shower. That fusion supports lower approach minima when properly equipped and authorized. And more importantly, it shrinks pilot workload on the nights when your payroll or your family is on board and the weather refuses to cooperate. Digital flight controls with careful envelope protection knit it all together. You feel the jet as precise, calm, and durable in the hands. Short, steep, and closer to where you're going. The Falcon 8X's landing manners are a quiet superpower. Approach speeds in the low hundreds of knots, low drag, high lift wizardry, and that disciplined tri-jet thrust picture add up to credible short-field and steep approach performance. The result is access, mountain airfields, coastal city airports tucked behind noise abatement rules, and financial districts that would otherwise force a 90-minute drive. There's a reason this airplane shows up again and again at places with tight numbers on the chart and big numbers on the contracts. Numbers you can feel in your bones. Specs make better sense when you translate them to experience. A maximum-certified altitude of 51,000 feet means you can climb over most weather structures, saving you from the bruising ride and the fuel burn that comes with step climbing under traffic. A maximum takeoff weight, a touch above 73,000 pounds, sounds abstract until you realize it buys you a full fuel load, a real cabin full of real people, and the confidence to skip a tech stop that would have cost an hour of your life. The cabin windows, 30 plus of them, are placed to land light, where your eyes actually want it. These are not accidental niceties. They are the fruits of people who fly thinking about how flying feels. Efficiency, maintenance, and the business case. Dassault's quiet obsession is efficiency. The 8X stretches farther on less fuel than many peers because of aerodynamic cleanliness, careful weight discipline, and engines optimized for crews rather than brochure numbers. Extended maintenance intervals, think inspection calendars measured in the hundreds of flight hours or a full year, translate directly into aircraft availability. For a flight department, availability is an ROI lever. Fewer days grounded by schedule. Fewer trips handed to charter as an expensive backup plan. Fewer surprises when the schedule looks like a chessboard. Operators talk in operating cost per hour and calendar days, but what they really buy is control. Safety philosophy, translated into confidence. Triple engines are not nostalgia. They're a risk calculus for complex global operations. The center engines S duct keeps the vertical tail clean. The outboards give you asymmetric authority without drama should you ever need it. Systems redundancy is layered through hydraulics, electrics, and avionics, so that single-point failures stay single-point. Add the flight control logic that prevents you from wandering into the corners of the envelope and a vision system that shows you the runway even when the atmosphere argues otherwise, and you have a jet that feels composed when the script goes unscripted. Real-world missions, real-world city pairs. Talk to crews who live in the airplane and a pattern emerges. The 8X connects Los Angeles to Geneva non-stop with reserves without asking everyone to bring a backpack instead of a suitcase. It will do New York to Dubai into headwinds that make other jets unfun. It will thread the needle into a steep approach European city, turn the passengers in three hours, and then launch across the Atlantic for the return while everyone on board is still having dinner at a sane local hour. The beauty is not just the range, but the repeatability of that range when the weather is sulking and the runways are shorter than corporate jets were supposed to need. The feel of the ride. Comfort is a hard thing to quantify, yet anyone who has flown an 8X can describe it. The cabin disappears in the sense that you stop noticing you're in a machine. The pressure feels benign. The acoustics are low enough that you pick a playlist for taste, not to drown out a frequency that bothers you. You can hold a natural voice across the aisle. You step off after 10 hours not wrung out, but collected, able to shake hands, present, or get to a childhood recital without that glassy post-flight stare. When a machine preserves your humanity after long hours aloft, that's luxury in its most honest form. Comparing the icons, why 8X still has a lane? In a world of headline-grabbing flagships, the Falcon 8X occupies a deliberate niche. Against the biggest twin-engined ultra-long-range jets, it seeds a little square footage, and in some cases, a few hundred miles of maximum brochure range. In exchange, it leans into runway access, refined handling, lower approach speeds, and a cabin pressure strategy that keeps passengers alert. It is, in many ways, the pilot's ultra-long-range jet, the one that makes sense when your destinations are not just megaports, with mile-long runways, but financial and resort airfields scattered closer to where life actually happens. If your calendar swings between global arcs and urban darts, the 8X's balance becomes hard to ignore. Economics, without the abrasive edges. Technical feel, in plain words. For those who like numbers told as a story, the fuselage gives you a cabin just over 42 feet long, 7 feet 8 inches wide, and a touch above 6 foot too high, with more than 30 windows spaced to bathe each zone in daylight. Pressurization keeps the cabin near 3,900 feet, while the aircraft cruises at 41,000, and the air is refreshed continuously, so a 12 hour leg doesn't feel like a 12 hour room. Maximum operating speed is Mach Decimal 9. Typical long range cruise is set for efficiency, not ego. Certified ceiling is 51,000 feet, opening a smooth lane over weather and traffic. Balanced field take off at maximum weight lives in the high 5,000s of feet, while unfactored landing distance at a typical weight, falls around 2,200. Three Pratt and Whitney Canada engines sit on the tail with about 6 and 3 quarter thousand pounds of thrust each, tuned not just for climb and cruise, but for the quiet, disciplined arrivals that end the day the way you'd want.

Here you can also see interesting&nbsp; features of the HondaJet. First of all, even though we&nbsp; have two batteries on board, the battery button is only one,&nbsp; it turns both batteries on. Then there's buttons to turn the generators on&nbsp; and off and the whole Honda's philosophy is, that if it's white and says OK or&nbsp; Norm, then it's works automatically. So if you want to turn one of the systems off, you&nbsp; press this button, it goes to a different state, for example if I turn the alternator&nbsp; off now it goes to the "off" mode. And I get a CAS message, we put the generator&nbsp; back on, the CAS message disappears. You can see from the CAS message the&nbsp; current state, that we have the door open, we know it's open and we see it in the picture,&nbsp; the cabin Door Open, External Power is also open, because we have an external power source&nbsp; connected, very convenient everything. Next. Here is nose wheel steering&nbsp; off. Why is it off? Because we are not going to steer now. This is the&nbsp; nose wheel control, before taxing. The gear control lever for the&nbsp; landing gear extension and retraction. Then we go to the center console, on the&nbsp; left side is the speed brakes control lever, they are on the tail, the flaps that open to the&nbsp; right and to the left and slow the airplane down. It seems to me that Honda has a very good&nbsp; solution because speed brakes which are usually on the wings create problems on the wing. They disrupt the flow, they reduce the wing aerodynamics and they add a lot&nbsp; of mechanics inside the wing. Tail speed brakes do not interrupt flow&nbsp; and flow over the wing remains laminar. I think this is interesting, but&nbsp; the main thing is that speed brakes with this configuration have no speed limits. In any configuration, whether you use flaps or not, you can release the speed brakes, it's cool. The right handle is the flaps control. Engines control. There is no autothrust, so you&nbsp; need to manually set the required engine power. But here is another interesting thing,&nbsp; this is Control Speed Cruise Control, CSC, works about the same way as cruise control&nbsp; in the car, you are flying at an altitude, you set the engine power, for example you need to&nbsp; keep the speed steady 340 or mach 0.62, you press cruise control and the aircraft tries to hold that&nbsp; speed by varying the engine power setting, but within certain limits since there is no autothrust&nbsp; here, and the levers stand still, plus or minus 5% is what the autopilot can add to the engine. So the speed is held. if the aircraft is unable to hold the set speed because the&nbsp; engine settings are not as stated, here the CSC light blinks and mode is off and I&nbsp; see on the panel that I have this mode turned off. Here, below the engine control knob, we&nbsp; have the controls for additional systems, particularly ice protection. As we mentioned during the preflight inspection, all systems on this&nbsp; aircraft work automatically, so when the indicator is in normal mode, this means that&nbsp; the antiice system will turn on automatically. What are these switches for, they are&nbsp; needed for the emergency modes. The de-icing is off now, but we can turn it on. Here we have fuel pump controls, here we have trims controls, Engine bleed, pneumatics and&nbsp; window heating, that's all about the environment. Next comes the magic, we now go up here. Cabin: Stepping inside the Pilatus PC twenty four,&nbsp; you’re greeted by a cabin that feels more like a&nbsp;&nbsp; luxurious living room than an aircraft. The cabin&nbsp; offers over five hundred cubic feet of space,&nbsp;&nbsp; significantly larger than other jets&nbsp; in its class. The flat-floor design&nbsp;&nbsp; ensures that even the tallest passengers&nbsp; have plenty of headroom and legroom,&nbsp;&nbsp; making long flights more comfortable. The attention to detail is evident in every&nbsp;&nbsp; inch of the cabin, from the plush seats to&nbsp; the state-of-the-art entertainment system&nbsp;&nbsp; that allows passengers to control cabin settings,&nbsp; watch movies, or track the flight—all from their&nbsp;&nbsp; personal devices. And let’s not forget about the&nbsp; baggage. With ninety cubic feet of baggage space,&nbsp;&nbsp; you can bring everything you need—and then some.&nbsp; Plus, the large cargo door at the rear of the&nbsp;&nbsp; plane makes loading and unloading a breeze. Don't forget to like this video, subscribe&nbsp;&nbsp; to our channel, and activate the&nbsp; notifications. Don't miss any of&nbsp;&nbsp; our videos about the world of luxury. The Pilatus PC twenty four is also a tech&nbsp;&nbsp; lover’s dream. The Williams engines&nbsp; are equipped with Quiet Power Mode,&nbsp;&nbsp; allowing one engine to operate at low speed to&nbsp; power the aircraft’s systems on the ground without&nbsp;&nbsp; the need for a noisy ground power unit. This&nbsp; not only makes for a quieter, more comfortable&nbsp;&nbsp; experience but also adds to the aircraft’s&nbsp; efficiency and operational flexibility. And speaking of efficiency, the jet’s advanced&nbsp; wing design and double-slotted flap system give&nbsp;&nbsp; it superior performance at both high and low&nbsp; speeds. This smart engineering allows the PC&nbsp;&nbsp; twenty four to handle everything from short&nbsp; runways to high-altitude airports with ease,&nbsp;&nbsp; making it a true all-terrain aircraft. Now, let’s turn our attention to the Phenom 300E,&nbsp;&nbsp; the upgraded version of Embraer’s best-selling&nbsp; Phenom 300. This jet is all about speed,&nbsp;&nbsp; sophistication, and cutting-edge avionics,&nbsp; making it the go-to choice for those who value&nbsp;&nbsp; performance and luxury. The Phenom three hundred E is the world’s fastest&nbsp; single-pilot jet, with a maximum operating speed&nbsp;&nbsp; of Mach zero-point-eight at thirty-three thousand&nbsp; feet. That’s about four hundred sixty-four miles&nbsp;&nbsp; per hour, making it the quickest aircraft in&nbsp; its class. But it’s not just about speed—the&nbsp;&nbsp; Phenom also boasts an impressive range of&nbsp; over two thousand nautical miles, meaning&nbsp;&nbsp; you can fly non-stop from New York to Miami or Los&nbsp; Angeles to Chicago without breaking a sweat. The aircraft’s time-to-climb is equally&nbsp; impressive, reaching its maximum altitude&nbsp;&nbsp; of forty-five thousand feet in just&nbsp; twenty-four minutes. This allows&nbsp;&nbsp; you to cruise above commercial air traffic,&nbsp; avoiding delays and turbulence for a smoother,&nbsp;&nbsp; more direct flight. When you step into the Phenom three hundred E,&nbsp; it’s clear that Embraer has pulled out all the&nbsp;&nbsp; stops to create a cabin experience that’s second&nbsp; to none. The new Bossa Nova interior option is a&nbsp;&nbsp; masterpiece of design, featuring custom stitching&nbsp; inspired by the sidewalks of Rio de Janeiro,&nbsp;&nbsp; piano black accents, and carbon fiber&nbsp; details that scream modern luxury. The cabin is not just about aesthetics—it’s also&nbsp; about comfort and convenience. The improved seat&nbsp;&nbsp; tracking gives pilots and passengers alike more&nbsp; legroom, while the enhanced noise insulation&nbsp;&nbsp; ensures that you’ll enjoy a quieter flight.&nbsp; The Phenom also features Gogo’s advanced L5&nbsp;&nbsp; air-to-ground connectivity system, which allows&nbsp; for fast internet speeds capable of streaming&nbsp;&nbsp; movies, sending emails with large attachments,&nbsp; and even making phone calls while in the air. And let’s talk about pressurization—the Phenom&nbsp; offers the best pressurization in its class,&nbsp;&nbsp; with a maximum cabin altitude of six thousand&nbsp; six hundred feet. This means you’ll feel more&nbsp;&nbsp; refreshed upon arrival, even after&nbsp; long flights at high altitudes. The Phenom three hundred E isn’t just fast—it’s&nbsp; smart, too. The Garmin G three thousand-based&nbsp;&nbsp; Prodigy Touch flight deck has been upgraded&nbsp; with new display hardware, faster processors,&nbsp;&nbsp; and improved map-panning features, giving pilots&nbsp; more control and better situational awareness. The&nbsp;&nbsp; system’s ability to import FMS speeds directly&nbsp; into the autopilot reduces pilot workload,&nbsp;&nbsp; making for a safer, more efficient flight. Safety is another area where the Phenom&nbsp;&nbsp; three hundred E shines. Embraer has introduced&nbsp; several new features, including the Runway&nbsp;&nbsp; Overrun Awareness and Alerting System, which&nbsp; helps prevent one of the most common causes&nbsp;&nbsp; of aviation accidents. By using real-time data&nbsp; to calculate the chances of a runway overrun,&nbsp;&nbsp; this system warns pilots if they’re&nbsp; approaching too fast or too steep,&nbsp;&nbsp; providing alerts that encourage timely action.&nbsp; The jet also features Emergency Descent Mode,&nbsp;&nbsp; which automatically flies the aircraft to a lower&nbsp; altitude in the event of cabin decompression,&nbsp;&nbsp; adding another layer of safety&nbsp; for passengers and crew. Head-to-Head Comparison: Now that we’ve explored the individual strengths&nbsp;&nbsp; of the Pilatus PC twenty four and the Phenom&nbsp; three hundred E, let’s put them head-to-head. When it comes to versatility, the Pilatus takes&nbsp; the crown. Its ability to operate on short,&nbsp;&nbsp; unpaved runways gives it access to more airports&nbsp; than any other jet in its class. This makes it the&nbsp;&nbsp; ideal choice for travelers who need to reach&nbsp; remote locations or prefer the convenience of&nbsp;&nbsp; landing closer to their final destination. On the other hand, if speed is your top priority,&nbsp;&nbsp; the Phenom is the clear winner. Its maximum&nbsp; speed of Mach zero-point-eight and range of&nbsp;&nbsp; over two thousand nautical miles make it the&nbsp; fastest and farthest-flying jet in the light&nbsp;&nbsp; jet category. This is the jet for those who&nbsp; want to get to their destination as quickly&nbsp;&nbsp; and efficiently as possible. When it comes to cabin comfort,&nbsp;&nbsp; both jets have their strengths. The Pilatus offers&nbsp; a larger cabin with more headroom and legroom,&nbsp;&nbsp; making it a great choice for long flights.&nbsp; However, the Phenom three hundred E’s Bossa&nbsp;&nbsp; Nova interior and advanced noise insulation create&nbsp; a more luxurious and quiet environment, perfect&nbsp;&nbsp; for those who want to travel in style. In terms of technology and safety, the Phenom&nbsp;&nbsp; edges out the competition. Its advanced avionics,&nbsp; coupled with features like Runway Overrun&nbsp;&nbsp; Awareness and Alerting System and Emergency&nbsp; Descent Mode, make it one of the safest and&nbsp;&nbsp; most technologically advanced jets in its class.&nbsp; The Pilatus PC twenty four, while no slouch in the&nbsp;&nbsp; tech department, doesn’t quite match the Phenom&nbsp; three hundred E’s cutting-edge systems. Now, let’s talk about something that’s just&nbsp; as important as performance, versatility,&nbsp;&nbsp; and comfort—the costs. Owning a private jet is&nbsp; a significant investment, and understanding the&nbsp;&nbsp; financial aspects is crucial when deciding between&nbsp; the Pilatus PC twenty four and the Phenom. Starting with the purchase price, the&nbsp; Pilatus PC twenty four typically comes&nbsp;&nbsp; in around ten-point-seven million dollars. On the&nbsp; other hand, the Phenom is priced slightly lower,&nbsp;&nbsp; with a base price of about nine-point-six&nbsp; million dollars. While both jets are in the&nbsp;&nbsp; upper range of the light jet market, the&nbsp; Phenom three hundred E offers a slightly&nbsp;&nbsp; more affordable entry point. When it comes to operating costs,&nbsp;&nbsp; the differences continue. The Pilatus has&nbsp; an estimated hourly operating cost of around&nbsp;&nbsp; one thousand eight hundred dollars. This includes&nbsp; fuel, maintenance, and other variable costs. The&nbsp;&nbsp; Phenom, while also economical for its class, comes&nbsp; in with a slightly higher hourly operating cost,&nbsp;&nbsp; averaging around one thousand nine hundred dollars&nbsp; per hour. This difference is largely due to the&nbsp;&nbsp; Phenom’s higher fuel consumption, driven&nbsp; by its greater speed and performance. If you’re considering offsetting some of your&nbsp; ownership costs by chartering out your jet,&nbsp;&nbsp; both the Pilatus and the Phenom three hundred&nbsp; E are popular choices in the charter market.&nbsp;&nbsp; The Phenom, with its speed and luxury, often&nbsp; commands higher charter rates, ranging from&nbsp;&nbsp; four thousand to five thousand dollars per hour,&nbsp; depending on the region. The Pilatus, with its&nbsp;&nbsp; unique versatility, can also fetch competitive&nbsp; rates, generally between three thousand and&nbsp;&nbsp; four thousand five hundred dollars per hour. So, which jet should you choose? It really comes&nbsp;&nbsp; down to what you value most&nbsp; in a private aircraft. If you’re looking for a jet&nbsp; that can take you anywhere,&nbsp;&nbsp; including remote locations that other jets can’t&nbsp; reach, the Pilatus is your best bet. Its versatility, combined with a spacious cabin&nbsp; and impressive performance, makes it the&nbsp;&nbsp; ultimate all-terrain private jet. However, if speed, luxury, and advanced&nbsp;&nbsp; technology are what you’re after, the Phenom&nbsp; is the jet for you. With its unmatched speed,&nbsp;&nbsp; sophisticated cabin, and top-of-the-line safety&nbsp; features, the Phenom three hundred E offers&nbsp;&nbsp; a flying experience that’s hard to beat. In the end, both the Pilatus PC twenty four and&nbsp;&nbsp; the Phenom three hundred E are incredible&nbsp; aircraft that represent the best in their class.&nbsp;&nbsp; Whether you prioritize versatility or speed,&nbsp; comfort or technology, there’s a jet here that’s&nbsp;&nbsp; perfect for your needs. - Inside the $10M Phenom 300E – Embraer’s Best-Selling Private Jet: Kind: captions Language: en It's fast, sleek, and packed with tech. And it's the bestselling private jet in the world for a reason. This is the $10 million Embraer Phenom 300E. A jet trusted by CEOs, charter companies, and firsttime owners alike. Today, we're stepping inside to see what makes it so popular and why it might be the smartest buy in private aviation. cabin. Step inside the Phenom 300E and the first thing that hits you is space. Lots of it. We're talking about a cabin that stretches 17 ft and 2 in from nose to tail, giving you more room to move around than most New York apartments. The width measures 4T and 11 in across, which means you can actually walk down the aisle without doing that awkward airplane shuffle. And with a height of 4 feet and 11 in, anyone under 6 feet can stand completely upright without ducking. But raw dimensions only tell half the story. What makes this cabin special is how Embraer uses every single inch. The standard configuration seats up to 11 passengers, but the real magic happens in the executive layouts. Picture this. Four captain's chairs that swivel, recline, and track forward and back. All upholstered in hand selected leather that probably costs more than most people's cars. These aren't airline seats with fancy covers. These are pieces of furniture that happen to be bolted into an aircraft. The cabin windows deserve their own spotlight. We're talking about the largest windows in the light jet class. Each one measuring 20 in tall by 14 in wide. That's 50% larger than the competition. which means when you're cruising over the Rocky Mountains or the Manhattan skyline, you're getting a view that makes every passenger feel like they have a window seat. Storage is where the Phenom 300E really flexes. The cabin features 67 cubic feet of interior storage, which translates to enough space for everyone's luggage, plus room for golf clubs, skis, or whatever toys successful people travel with. There's also an external baggage compartment with 84 cubic feet of space, meaning you're not choosing between passengers and cargo. The cabin technology package reads like something out of a science fiction movie. Every seat gets its own LED reading light, power outlets, and USB ports. The cabin management system lets passengers control lighting, temperature, and entertainment from their smartphones. The sound system pumps crystalclear audio through strategically placed speakers, while the cabin pressure system keeps you feeling fresh even on long flights by maintaining a cabin altitude of just 6,000 ft when cruising at 45,000. Cockpit. Slide into the cockpit of the Phenom 300E and you're entering one of the most advanced flight decks in aviation. The Prodigy Touchflight Deck powered by Garmin transforms flying from a technical challenge into an intuitive experience. We're talking about four highresolution touchscreen displays that make controlling this aircraft feel more like operating a luxury car than flying a jet. The primary flight displays measure 12 in diagonally and deliver information with stunning clarity. Every piece of data a pilot needs. Altitude, airspeed, heading, navigation, weather, radar, terrain warnings, bees appears exactly where it should, when it should. The touchscreen interface eliminates the need for hundreds of traditional switches and knobs, replacing them with intuitive menus that respond instantly to pilot commands. But here's where it gets really impressive. The automatic emergency descent system. If cabin pressure drops unexpectedly, this system takes over without pilot input, automatically descending the aircraft to a safe altitude, and navigating to the nearest suitable airport. It's like having a co-pilot that never gets tired, never gets distracted, and never makes mistakes. The synthetic vision technology deserves special mention. This system creates a three-dimensional view of terrain, obstacles, and runways, even in zero visibility conditions. Pilots can literally see through clouds, fog, and darkness using this technology. Combined with enhanced flight vision systems that use infrared sensors, the Phenom 300E can operate safely in weather conditions that would ground other aircraft. Weather radar coverage extends out to 320 nautical miles, giving pilots plenty of advanced warning about storms, turbulence, and other weather hazards. The system automatically scans for threats and highlights dangerous areas in vivid colors, making weather avoidance as simple as steering around the red zones on the display. Stick around until the end to discover the hidden costs that most Phenom 300E owners never see coming. Plus, the secret upgrade that transforms this already impressive jet into something truly extraordinary. Engine and performance. Under the sleek exterior of the Phenom 300E beats the heart of performance. Two Pratt and Whitney Canada PW535E engines. These power plants don't just move the aircraft, they launch it. Each engine produces 3,047 lb of thrust, giving this jet a thrusttoe ratio that puts it in the same league as aircraft costing twice as much. The numbers tell the story of serious performance. Maximum cruise speed hits 526 mph at 45,000 ft, making this one of the fastest jets in its class. But speed without range is just showing off. The Phenom 300E delivers both. With four passengers aboard, this jet can fly 2,018 nautical miles non-stop. That means non-stop flights from New York to Denver, Los Angeles to Chicago or Miami to Boston without breaking a sweat. What really impresses aviation enthusiasts is the service ceiling of 45,000 ft. This isn't just about bragging rights. Flying at this altitude means staying above most weather, reducing turbulence, and shortening flight times by taking advantage of jet streams. Most light jets top out at 41,000 ft, but the Phenom 300E climbs higher, literally rising above the competition. The takeoff and landing performance opens up airports that other jets simply can't access. The Phenom 300E needs just 3,599 ft of runway for takeoff and 2560 ft for landing. This performance unlocks access to over 5,000 airports worldwide, including challenging destinations like Aspen, Colorado, and Teter, New Jersey. Price. Let's talk numbers. The kind that matter when you're writing checks with multiple zeros. The base price of a new Embraer Phenom 300E starts at approximately $10.2 million. But that's just the entry ticket to the club. By the time most buyers finish customizing their aircraft with premium interiors, advanced avionics options, and personalized features, the final price typically lands between 11 and $13 million. But ownership costs extend far beyond the initial purchase price. Annual operating expenses for a Phenom 300E typically run between 1.8 and $2.2 million per year, assuming 250 flight hours annually. This includes fuel, insurance, maintenance, pilot salaries, hanger fees, and regulatory compliance costs. For those not ready to commit to ownership, charter rates for the Phenom 300E typically range from 4,500 to $6,000 per flight hour depending on location, season, and availability. Peak travel times like holidays and major events can push rates even higher, sometimes reaching $7,000 per hour or more. The hidden costs that catch many new owners offguard include positioning flights when the aircraft isn't where you need it, international handling fees that can reach thousands of dollars per trip, and upgrade costs for aging avionic systems. Embraer regularly releases software updates and hardware improvements, and staying current with these advances can cost hundreds of thousands of dollars over the aircraft's lifetime. But for those who can afford the investment, the Phenom 300E delivers an ownership experience that justifies every dollar. This isn't just about getting from point A to point B. It's about arriving refreshed on your schedule with your privacy intact and your productivity maximized. In a world where time is the ultimate currency, the Phenom 300E doesn't just buy you transportation, it buys you back the most precious commodity of all. - New 2025 Jet Embraer Phenom 300E - The Most Luxurious Aircraft & Best Seller in the World!: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] The Embraer Phenom 300E is one of the most advanced light business jets on the market. Known for its combination of speed, efficiency, and luxury. Manufactured by Brazilian aerospace company Embraer, the Phenom 300E is an enhanced version of the successful Phenom 300. It features improved avionics, improved performance, and a redesigned cabin for greater passenger comfort. As the bestselling light jet for several years, it remains a popular choice among corporate executives, private owners, and charter companies looking for a high performance aircraft with intercontinental capabilities. In terms of performance, the Phenom 300E stands out with a top speed of 464 knots, Mach 0.80, and a range of of 2010 nautical miles with six passengers. Powered by two Pratt Whitney Canada PW535 E1 engines, the aircraft offers superior fuel efficiency, reducing operating costs while maintaining impressive power. Its maximum cruising altitude is 45,000 ft, allowing it to fly above most commercial air traffic, resulting in a smoother and more efficient flight. Its short takeoff and landing distances also make it ideal for accessing smaller airports, increasing travel flexibility. One of the most compelling features of the Phenom 300E is its advanced avionic suite. The jet is equipped with a Garmin G3000 Prodigy Touch flight deck, which enhances pilot situational awareness with intuitive touchscreen controls, a synthetic vision system, and auto throttle technology. These enhancements reduce pilot workload, making the Phenom 300E one of the safest and most modern light jets available. Additionally, the Phenom 300E is equipped with Embrier's runway overrun awareness and alerting system, Euro AAS, a safety feature unique to the Phenom 300 series. The Phenom 300's cabin is designed for the utmost in comfort and luxury. Embrier has optimized the interior with the Bosanova design package, which offers premium materials, ergonomic seating, and a flat floor cabin for enhanced mobility. The aircraft can accommodate up to 10 passengers in a spacious and quiet environment with advanced climate control and adjustable lighting. The aircraft is also equipped with a sophisticated in-flight entertainment system and high-speed connectivity allowing passengers to stay productive or relaxed during the flight. - How much does it cost to fly private? - Rachel Yang: Kind: captions Language: en Across 2023 and 2024, one internationally renowned musician flew their private jet 396 times in 365 days. And one of those trips was just six minutes long. Figures like this aren’t uncommon among the world’s richest jet-setters— in fact, this frequent flyer probably isn’t even the celebrity you’re thinking of. So what's the price tag for this high flying lifestyle? The majority of private flying happens in the US, where even a relatively short trip is incredibly expensive. If you want to fly private, you have two options: buying a jet or renting one. Jet rentals can run from roughly $7,500 an hour for domestic flights to roughly $17,000 an hour for international ones. For context, that’s over $40,000 to fly from LA to New York, and over $120,000 to fly from New York to London. And that's before paying an additional several thousand dollars for the crew, the fuel, landing fees for the airport, and federal taxes. That said, these rental costs are still much cheaper than actually buying a plane. Depending on their size, new jets run anywhere from $2 to $65 million. Then, in addition to the usual operational costs for each flight, jet owners still have to pay for a hangar to park in and up to $1 million a year for routine inspections and maintenance. This is already absurdly expensive. But private flying also has a major environmental cost. While both private and commercial planes emit a lot of CO2, commercial planes do so to move a lot of people. For example, in 2022, commercial airplanes emitted roughly 800 megatons of CO2 moving about 853 million passengers. That’s a little less than one ton of CO2 per person per flight. On average, most people emit four tons of CO2 a year— so taking two round trip flights would double their emissions. This math already isn’t great, but private jets make it so much worse. With only a handful of people onboard, private flights emit 10 to 20 times more emissions per passenger on average. And since their passengers tend to be frequent flyers, their individual emissions are astronomical. In 2023 alone, the top ten US private fliers produced more combined emissions than 6,500 people. And for the past few years, roughly 1% of fliers have been responsible for almost half of aviation emissions. This discrepancy is huge, and it will likely continue growing as the private aviation industry expands. However, while we probably can’t convince celebrities and CEOs to fly commercial, one promising approach for reducing plane emissions is swapping existing jet fuel for Sustainable Aviation Fuels, or SAF. Traditional jet fuel is made from fossil fuels and releases carbon into the atmosphere when burned. SAFs, on the other hand, are made from waste materials like used cooking oil, and renewable crops like corn and algae. These biomaterials still emit carbon when burned, but growing these crops pulls enough CO2 out of the atmosphere to offset those emissions by up to 86%. If the entire jet fuel pipeline shifted to SAFs, it could reduce aviation emissions by up to 94%. But for this to happen, SAF producers need to overcome two obstacles. First, there aren't enough crops to produce biofuels on a large scale. Researchers are developing synthetic SAFs that don’t require crops, but they’re still being rolled out. Second, current SAF production is relatively small scale, which makes it extremely expensive. At three times the cost of normal fuel, most airlines won’t even consider making the switch, and if they did it would cause airfare to skyrocket. Governments in Singapore, Japan, and most notably the EU have already started programs to support widespread SAF adoption and fund their development. But this is where private fliers could actually help the environment. Most jet owners already spend hundreds of thousands of dollars a year on private flights, and it would only cost them a few thousand more per flight to use synthetic SAFs. This boom in usage would fund both SAF development and large-scale production. And those advances would make sustainable fuel cheaper for everyone— both the people flying private and those privately wishing they were.

- The Economics of Flying Private: How NetJets Became the Uber of Private Jets: Kind: captions Language: en What does it actually cost to fly private? And why are more people doing it than ever before? Private aviation is a multibillion dollar business. And companies like NetJets are changing how the ultra wealthy move. Even in the smallest aircraft type in our program, you're talking today about an initial capital investment of more than $800,000 to buy a piece of an airplane. We have something like 40% of the Fortune 500 that are are NetJets customers. And as major commercial airlines are trying to win back premium customers, can commercial aviation companies successfully keep them? [Music] NetJets doesn't sell private jets. It sells fractions of jets. Customers buy a share as little as 116th of a specific aircraft model. That idea splitting jet ownership like a time share unlocked a new market. Today we operate nearly,00 aircraft which if you were comparing us to a commercial airline would make us the second or third largest on the planet. Unlike charter services, it owns and controls its fleet allowing tighter control over quality scheduling and safety. The average NetJets customer flies with us for about 75 to 100 hours per year. Our typical customer will be somebody who is a principal of a privately held company. We have something like 40% of the Fortune 500 that are our NetJets customers. Flying with NetJets doesn't come cheap. A fractional ownership share on a light jet like the Phenom 300 starts at around $850,000 upfront, plus about $325,000 per year in operating costs for 50 flight hours. For those who want a more affordable and flexible option, the NetJets card starts at $215,000 for 25 hours annually. That $215,000 jet card, that's the cost of four down payments on the average American home. Within the shared ownership model, we really have three cost components for the customer. You have the initial capital investment, which is a onetime investment where somebody's buying a piece of the airplane and then they pay a monthly management fee. If you owned your own airplane, you'd have pilot salaries, you need to have mechanic salaries, and you'd have hanger rent. So in our model, you're sharing that across the other members of the program. And then every time you fly, we bill you the variable hourly costs associated with flying that trip from A to B, which covers things like the fuel expense of the aircraft, the ground charges and the handling fees and the things that that would go into operating an airplane from A to B. That business model caught the attention of Warren Buffett. And in 1998, Berkshire Hathaway bought NetJets and has held on to it since. You see in the luxury travel segment overall, people are making more of an investment in experiences than in things. That certainly has been a tailwind for us. You know, if I'm being honest, the commercial experience has not gotten any better over the last several years, and that has driven more people to want to fly netjets. During the pandemic, demand for private air travel soared as people feared crowded airports and packed planes. In the US, business aviation traffic in 2021 hit record highs, surpassing the 2019 levels, and in 2021 saw over 3.3 million private jets worldwide, a 7% increase over preandemic levels. Only about 10% of the people who met the financial, you know, norms or averages of private aviation were actually flying privately. and CO certainly unlocked some portion of that addressable market. And then the big question was would they go back to the airlines when the pandemic subsided and you know masks came off and vaccines went in arms. After the initial co boom many private airline companies saw slumps in profits but in Q1 of 2025 Berkshire Hathaway reported a profit bump in its service division which it contributed to its aviation services. For NetJets, we're operating at 40 45% larger than we were in 2019. So, we've maintained record retention rates. Major commercial airlines are trying to claw back premium travelers by rolling out redesigned business class cabins, faster boarding, and high-end lounges. But for some flyers, the best perks aren't enough. Anonymity is key to our business. One of the appeals of flying netjets versus owning your own airplane is that you're virtually untraceable. Whether you have concerns about appearances or whether you have concerns about safety, netjets can provide that in a way that that you can't get owning your own airplane. As the demand for private flights increases, so does the visibility of their impact. Each trip emits many times more carbon emissions per passenger than a commercial trip, and that's drawn growing scrutiny, especially as more celebrities and CEOs are called out for frequent short hall trips. When Taylor Swift's private jet usage was tracked online in 2023, it sparked a wave of backlash and highlighted the growing divide between convenience and climate responsibility. Now with the business booming, even the industry's biggest players are under pressure to address the carbon footprint. Sustainability is at the forefront for our industry right now. And the way that we're addressing it at NetIts is we are the largest buyer of sustainable aviation fuel in the industry. We offer carbon offset programs for our customers. We offset all of our own internal company flying. So anything that we do for maintenance flights or training flights or anything like that, we we buy the carbon credits to offset. As commercial travel faces continued pressure from rising costs to growing delays, demand for more efficient discrete options isn't likely to vanish. Private aviation remains a niche but profitable business fueled by clients who value control more than cost.
