reputable private jet Company by searching online or at a local airport once you have found a company that meets your needs you can request a flight quote there are two types of private jet companies operators and brokerages operators are companies that physically maintain and fly the aircraft while brokerages are intermediaries between customers operators when booking a flight operators will typically quote their own aircraft if the flight is out of an airport where they have planes based if their aircraft is unavailable or the flight is outside their normal coverage Zone operators will call other operators to check availability and book off Fleet options for their clients on the other hand Brokers rely solely on outside operators to provide the aircraft and often do not have a direct connection to the aircraft they are working with the choice between an operator and a broker comes down to personal preference some customers prefer to cut out the middlemen by going straight to the operator While others do not mind going through a broker an operator can also act as a broker for a client if they do not have a plane available for a requested mission well for frequent Travelers there are options to make the booking process more efficient for paying for flights by purchasing a jet card opting into a membership-based program or purchasing fractional ownership of an aircraft can be beneficial in the long run for occasional Flyers an on-demand Charter allows all the perks of flying private without a long-term investment it's also an excellent option for those who want to experience private jet travel without a long-term investment commitment the bottom line is it by understanding the options asking the right questions and carefully considering your needs the process of booking a private jet can be simple and stress-free step two determine the ideal aircraft when calling a private jet charter operator or brokerage it's essential to be prepared with key information in order to ensure a smooth and efficient booking process the charter representative will typically ask several important questions including your destination departure and return dates the number of passengers preferred aircraft size luggage requirements and budget these questions will help to determine the best aircraft option for your specific needs the number of passengers can play a significant role in determining the minimum size of the aircraft required for example a group of nine people will likely require a super mid-size aircraft while a group of only two people can comfortably fit into a turboprop or light jet Additionally the amount in size of luggage can also impact the size of the aircraft needed as certain oversized items like golf clubs skis and large suitcases may not fit in every private jet overall it's important to have an understanding of your specific needs and preferences including passenger count luggage requirements and budget in order to ensure that you select the right private jet charter firm and aircraft for your trip clear communication with the charter representative will make the process easy and ensure a comfortable luxurious flight step 3 evaluate the safety standards when booking a private jet charter you must ensure that you fly with a safe aircraft and crew not all operators have the same safety standards so it's crucial to ask about the safety ratings of the aircraft crew and operator safety ratings in private Aviation are determined by two major companies Argus and wyvern these ratings indicate that a safety audit has been performed and that the flight Crews meet certain safety thresholds to ensure a high level of safety clients should request a crew of two pilots who meet either Argus or wyvern safety standards step 4 booking and paying for the charter once clients have provided all the necessary information for their desired trip including any specific safety ratings the charter representative will present them with various aircraft options these options typically include information about the aircraft type available amenities and the final cost once the client has selected their preferred aircraft they will be sent a contract to review and sign which outlines the terms and conditions of the flight as well as any cancellation or change policies in place the contract can typically be signed electronically using software like DocuSign once it is signed the flight is considered locked in payment for the flight is typically required before the day of departure and can be made in various ways the most common method is a wire transfer but some companies may also accept payments via credit card or check depending on the preferences in addition to the flight cost any optional extras that the client has requested such as catering or ground transportation will be added to the final bill these extras are usually built separately from the flight itself so the client can clearly see the cost breakdown of their trip step 5 procedural check once the trip is booked and an aircraft is assigned the charter sales representative or broker will provide the client with a detailed itinerary also known as a trip sheet this itinerary will contain three important pieces of information the tail number of the aircraft the departure facility and the departure time well the tail number is a unique identification code assigned to the aircraft which is used to ensure that the charter group is boarding the correct plane unlike commercial airlines private charter flights do not have flight numbers so the tail number is the primary identification method the departure facility will typically be a private terminal located at an airport also known as a fixed Base operator FBO these facilities are usually smaller than commercial Terminals and offer amenities such as lounges chairs bathrooms and in some cases additional services like a popcorn machine or golf simulator one of the benefits of departing from an FBO is that passengers can drive right up to the aircraft and at many airports they can even have their car valid if they arrive early they usually have free parking and a short walk from the parking lot to the plane once inside the passengers will present their tail number to the front desk representative who will connect them with their pilots or flight crew a line service technician will take care of their baggage and transport them directly to the plane after a quick identification check and pre-flight briefing the passengers will board the plane and the trip can begin step 6 in-flight amenities The Experience on board a chartered flight will vary depending on several factors such as the type of air crowd any additional services that have been pre-ordered and whether a cabin attendant is present most aircraft will provide a basic selection of snacks and drinks including non-alcoholic and alcoholic options if a passenger has pre-ordered catering the food will be set up in the aircraft's galley for them to enjoy during the flight if a cabin attendant is on board they will personally serve food and drinks to passengers the level of in-flight entertainment will also depend on the type of aircraft larger aircraft usually have more advanced entertainment systems such as DVD players audio systems and sometimes even Wi-Fi however it's advisable to confirm the availability of such amenities before departure with the charter representative step 7 post flight formalities when arriving at the destination many private charter flights will offer the convenience of having transportation arranged and waiting for the passengers at the arrival FBO plain side some passengers prefer to use ride hailing services like Uber or other Private Car Services it's important to note that keeping the aircraft clean during and after the flight is the passenger's responsibility if the aircraft requires professional cleaning after a flight the passenger may be charged for it if the same aircraft is being used for the return flight the flight crew may offer to provide the passengers with their personal contact information such as a phone number so that they can be reached directly in case of any changes to the flight schedule While most changes will still need to be made through the charter representative having the Pilot's phone number can help coordinate any last minute changes or updates so this concludes all the necessary information to understand the process of chartering a private jet and this brings us to the end of our video click the links to check out some of our other videos moreover don't forget to like share and subscribe for more content like this we have many great videos coming up see you in the next video

Do we cut corners and fly around in a less capable, less safe Air Force One?" Here in the US, the rise of private flying is closely tied with the history of Air Force One. Franklin Roosevelt became the first president to fly in a private purpose-built plane in 1945 when he traveled to Crimea during World War II. Roosevelt's plane had a conference room, a special wheelchair lift, a bulletproof window, and a refrigerator, the epitome of luxury at the time. In 1953, President Dwight Eisenhower became the first to officially call his plane the Air Force One, when he upgraded to a jet, a Boeing 707. 10 years later, in 1963, a Swiss American aviation company, Learjet, introduced the first private aircraft with seats for six passengers and two pilots. Its price tag started at about $650,000. In the late '7s, Boeing began converting its popular 747 model for private luxury travel. Over the next two decades, charter services emerged, allowing people to easily explore and book private trips, ushering in a new era of flying. [Applause] Qatar has been building its own aviation empire since 1993 while using planes as a diplomacy tool. In 2000, the country sent a Boeing 747 to Iraq as an expression of solidarity. In 2013, when Turkish and Lebanese hostages were released from Syria, Qatar executive quickly offered jets to escort them home. And in 2018, Qatar's Amir gifted a Boeing 747 to Turkish President Arduan, citing his trust in Turkey. I don't think there's any question that they regard aviation as a kind of an integrated well fabric if you will, economics, diplomacy, uh, military power, so hard and soft power. They there's no way they could be spending this monster amount of money on jets without having a sense of what it all means. >> Becoming an aviation powerhouse has also helped Qatar secure its position in the Middle East. When tensions escalated between Qatar and its neighbors in 2017 over its ties to Iran, Egypt, the UAE, Bahrain, and Saudi Arabia imposed a massive blockade. They denied the tiny kingdom access to their airspace and seas and closed its only land border. Qatar relied on imports for survival and the country was facing a crisis. It quickly turned to its aviation superiority to flex some muscle. The airline flew thousands of dairy cows on Qatar Airways cargo planes from Germany, the Netherlands, the US, and Australia to Doha, where a new farm was waiting to help give the country more control over its food supply. >> Aviation was their lifeline. And whether economically or diplomatically, there was that high-profile story, I believe, of using Qatari jetliners to fly dairy cows into the country so they could have milk, you know. I mean, it was it was an exceptional moment in history. >> And Qatar's influence in aviation extends beyond just owning fancy planes. The Qatar Investment Authority, the country's $500 billion sovereign wealth fund, has bought shares in other airlines. In 2025, it scooped up a 25% stake in Virgin Australia after Australia wouldn't allow Qatar Airways to expand its flights. In 2020, Qatar secured its 25% stake in IA, which operates British Airways, Iberia, and Heirlingus among others. And back in 2012, the country bought a 20% stake in London's Heathro airport. It also owns part of two African airlines, Airlink and Rwanda Air. Qatar's ties to the United States also run deep. It shelled out $8 billion over the last 20 years to help develop and maintain the Al- Uid air base, the largest US military facility in the Middle East. It houses some 10,000 troops. In May 2025, Trump said Qatar pledged another $10 billion towards the base over the coming years. These closed ties benefit some US manufacturers as well, like Boeing. Qatar Airways has an order for 210 aircraft. And Qatar Executive flies 22 private jets made by another American manufacturer, Gulfream, with a few more scheduled for delivery next year. The private jet business is also growing worldwide. Today, there are about 23,000 private jets operating around the world, and about 65% of those are based in the US. That's up from 10,000 jets operating globally in 2000. Analysts say the global business jet market could reach nearly $70 billion by 2032. But owning a private jet isn't the only option. Some invest in fractional ownership, kind of like a time share for jets, where owners share costs such as maintenance, staff salaries, and hanger space. Others book and share chartered planes, or they use jet card programs that are like a prepaid card for flight hours. Vista Jet is one company that offers such a program. >> As long as the runway is long and safe enough, we'll make it happen. >> Vista Jet started with just two Lear jets in 2004. Now it has a fleet of more than 300 aircraft around the world. The company uses a subscription-based plan in which clients buy flight hours and it replicates ownership. But what it does is that it takes away all the ownership risks where the asset depreciation, the the management of the aircraft and the operation of the aircraft. >> It's a convenience clients are willing to pay for. The flight time costs anywhere from 11,000 to about 25,000 an hour depending on the type of plane. Members have to purchase a minimum of 25 flight hours a year with a 3-year commitment. And there's a separate fuel charge on top of the hourly rates. One flight from New York to London on this aircraft type could cost you $300,000 where our average client average net worth are well over half a billion dollars. members are guaranteed a plane will be available for their flight as long as they give 24 hours notice. >> It's like doing a jigsaw puzzle. How you make sure that you have enough availability, but at the same time, we can't have too much waste. >> To avoid empty seats, the company also sells individual chartered flights outside the membership program. And they're looking for ways to reduce their carbon footprint. Studies have found that private flights are up to 14 times more polluting per passenger than flying commercial as a carbon footprint is spread across fewer people. One study calculated that 2 hours of a private flight emits more carbon dioxide than an average person creates in a year. And because these planes fly higher, the emissions are released in more sensitive parts of the atmosphere where they linger, potentially contributing to more global warming. 85% of our customers opt in for our carbon emission program. - Exploring the Falcon 7X & 8X: How Dassault Defines High-End Aviation: Kind: captions Language: en The Dissult Falcon 7X and 8X are two of the most advanced and luxurious business jets in the world. Combining DALT Aviation's military-grade engineering with high-end comfort, these jets redefine private aviation with their innovative trienine design, extended range, and cuttingedge avionics, making them a top choice for business leaders and governments. In this video, we'll explore how the 7X revolutionized business aviation and how the 8X pushed the boundaries even further with better performance, a refined cabin, and improved efficiency. From their powerful PW engines to their ultra luxurious interiors, we'll break down what sets these jets apart and why they remain some of the best private aircraft available today. Give Luxo Life some love and let's get started. The evolution of the Falcon series. The first assault Falcon 7X and 8X didn't just appear out of nowhere. They evolved from a long legacy of innovation in business aviation. Before these jets took center stage, the Falcon 900 was Dissult's top business aircraft. It was a solid performer. But as technology advanced and competition from Bombardier and Gulfream grew stronger, it became clear that Dissult needed something more powerful, more efficient, and with better range. The Falcon 900 had served its purpose. But to stay ahead, DEALT had to push forward with a game-changing upgrade. That upgrade came in 2001 with the Falcon 7X, a jet that redefined what a private business aircraft could do. This wasn't just an improved Falcon 900. It was an entirely new aircraft. The 7X introduced a flybywire control system, something usually found in fighter jets, making it more precise and safer to fly. It also had a longer range, a more spacious cabin, and improved aerodynamics. Dissult took its expertise in military aviation and applied it to the world of luxury travel, and the results were outstanding. By 2010, over a 100 Falcon 7X jets had been delivered, proving that DEALT had made the right move. But DEALT didn't stop there. In 2014, they unveiled the Falcon 8X, taking everything great about the 7X and making it even better. The 8X had a longer range, meaning it could fly farther without refueling, making it ideal for long haul business travel. The cabin was redesigned for more comfort, better soundproofing, and upgraded luxury features. And the aircraft itself was more fuelefficient without sacrificing performance. It wasn't just a minor upgrade. It was a major leap forward that solidified DELT's place at the top of the private jet industry. Design and performance comparison. Falcon 7X versus Falcon 8X. When it comes to design and performance comparison, Falcon 7X versus Falcon 8X may look similar at first glance, but Dissult made some key changes to take the 8X to the next level. Both jets share the sleek aerodynamic look that defines the Falcon series. But the 8X is slightly longer at 24.46 m compared to the 7X's 23.38 m. This extra length gives the 8X more cabin space and improves stability in flight. Dissult also fine-tuned the wing design, making the 8X's wings even more efficient, reducing drag for better fuel economy and smoother handling. These upgrades make the 8X not just an improvement, it's a smarter, more capable aircraft. A standout feature of both jets is the three engine configuration. Something you don't see often in business jets today. While many manufacturers have moved to twin engine setups, the Salt stuck with three engines for a reason. The trijet layout allows for shorter takeoff distances, making it easier to operate from challenging airports with shorter runways. It also provides an extra layer of safety over long flights, especially when crossing vast oceans or remote areas. This setup isn't just about nostalgia. It gives the 7X and 8X a real operational advantage that twin engine competitors can't match. Beyond design, performance is where the Falcon 8X takes the lead. Thanks to its upgraded engines and aerodynamic improvements, the 8X can fly farther. 11,945 km compared to the 7X's 11,019 km. That extra range means fewer fuel stops and more direct flights, which is a huge advantage for business travelers who value efficiency. The 8X is also slightly faster, reaching speeds of Mach 0.90 compared to the 7X's Mach0.87. It might not seem like a huge difference, but in private aviation, every bit of extra speed and efficiency counts. The power behind the performance. PW300 engines. Now, let's talk about what keeps the Falcon 7X and 8X soaring. their Pratt and Whitney PW37 engines. Dissult had plenty of options when choosing an engine, but they went with the PW37 series for a reason. These engines offer a perfect balance of power, efficiency, and reliability, making them an ideal choice for long haul business jets. The 7X is equipped with PW37A engines, while the 8X features upgraded PW37D engines, giving it more thrust and better fuel efficiency. DALT wanted an engine that could deliver strong performance while keeping operating costs low, and Pratt and Whitney delivered. One of the biggest advantages of the PW37D engines in the Falcon 8X is fuel efficiency. Compared to the 7X, the 8X consumes less fuel thanks to its improved aerodynamics and engine refinements. This means lower operating costs and a longer range without needing extra fuel stops. With a maximum range of 11,945 km, the Falcon 8X can handle long haul flights with ease, making it a top choice for executives and world leaders who need to travel efficiently. Less fuel burn also means a smaller carbon footprint, which is an increasingly important factor in modern aviation. The PW37 engines also improve operational flexibility, giving both the Falcon 7X and 8X the ability to take off from shorter runways and challenging airports. Their three engine configuration provides extra security during long oceanic flights, reducing restrictions on where these jets can operate. Whether it's a high alitude airport, a shorter private airirstrip, or a remote destination, the PW37 powered Falcons can handle it all. This flexibility is a huge advantage over twin engine competitors, making the Salts Falcons some of the most versatile private jets on the market. Luxury and comfort. Inside the Falcon 7X and 8X. The comfort inside the Falcon 7X and 8X is what truly sets these jets apart. Dissult didn't just focus on performance. They made sure passengers travel in absolute luxury. Both jets have a spacious, customizable cabin, but the Falcon 8X takes it a step further. Thanks to its longer fuselage, the 8X offers more layout options, including additional seating, a larger dining area, or even a private office. Every detail inside is designed for comfort, productivity, and relaxation, making these jets feel more like a luxury penthouse in the sky rather than just an aircraft. One of the biggest improvements in the Falcon 8X cabin is sound insulation. Dissult worked hard to make the 8X one of the quietest private jets in the world, allowing passengers to relax, work, or sleep undisturbed. The cabin is also pressurized at a lower altitude, which helps reduce fatigue on long flights. Whether you're flying for a few hours or crossing the ocean, the experience inside the 8X is smoother, quieter, and more comfortable than ever. For entertainment, the Sky Lounge multimedia system takes the experience to another level. This high-end system gives passengers full control over in-flight entertainment, lighting, and climate settings. all from their personal devices. Whether you want to watch a movie, attend a video conference, or adjust the mood lighting, everything is at your fingertips. And if luxury is your priority, the Falcon 8X offers something rare in private jets. A full bathroom with a vertical shower, ensuring you arrive at your destination feeling completely refreshed. The cockpit, advanced avionics, and flybywire technology. When it comes to the cockpit of the Falcon 7X and 8X, Bissalt built an advanced control center that gives pilots unmatched precision and efficiency. One of the standout features is the flybywire system, a technology originally developed for fighter jets. Unlike traditional mechanical controls, flybywire replaces manual inputs with electronic signals, making the aircraft smoother, more responsive, and safer to fly. Dissult was the first to introduce this technology in a business jet with the Falcon 7X, and the 8X refineses it even further, giving pilots more control with less effort. A key part of the Falcon 8X cockpit is its large panoramic windows designed to improve visibility and situational awareness. Whether flying at night, through bad weather, or into challenging airports, pilots get a clearer, more natural view of their surroundings. This design not only enhances safety but also reduces fatigue, making long haul flights easier to manage. Combined with an ergonomic layout and touchscreen displays, the cockpit ensures that pilots have everything they need at their fingertips for a smooth and efficient flight. Automation also plays a huge role in the Falcon 7X and 8X. These jets are packed with advanced avionics and safety systems that assist pilots in navigation, weather monitoring, and fuel management. The Falconiz system, for example, offers enhanced vision technology, allowing pilots to see clearly in low visibility conditions. With intelligent automation handling routine tasks, pilots can focus on what matters most, delivering a safe and seamless flight experience. The Falcon 7X and 8X prove why Dissult remains a leader in high-end aviation. Combining performance, efficiency, and luxury in a way few jets can match. While the 7X revolutionized business aviation, the 8X takes it even further with better range, fuel efficiency, and a refined cabin experience. But now, we want to hear from you. Would you choose the Falcon 7X or the Falcon 8X? Drop your thoughts in the comments. The PC12 runs on the trusted Pratt and Whitney PT6A. It's efficient for its class, but jet A is never cheap. The engine burns around 60 to 70 gall hour. If you fly roughly 300 hours a year and jet A averages $6 per gallon, your fuel bill alone lands near $126,000. That's before you even consider maintenance or crew. Maintenance. Maintenance is where reality hits. Every 100 hours, you're due for a basic inspection. At 61,200 hours, the checks become major events. A 1,200hour inspection often costs more than $100,000. Then comes the hot section inspection on the PT6, usually around the $2,000 hour mark. Even though it's not a full overhaul, it can still run $250,000 or more. If you don't plan for this, it feels like a financial explosion. The hidden cost that crushes firsttime owners dot fuel and maintenance are predictable. The real shock comes from something owners don't think about at first, crew and management. You can't realistically own a multi-million dollar aircraft without a professional pilot and proper oversight. A skilled PC12 pilot earns between 90,000 and $150,000 a year. Add benefits, annual training, hotels, and travel costs, and the number rises quickly. If you use an aircraft management company to handle scheduling, compliance, and maintenance tracking, that's another yearly fee added on top. These two expenses alone push ownership costs up before the plane even leaves the hanger. Your total comes to roughly $446,000 per year. with real world variations. That $500,000 figure isn't exaggerated at all. It's simply the entry fee to PC12 ownership. PC12 versus Citation Jet. Which one really costs less? Now to the big comparison. Is the PC12 cheaper to run per hour than a light jet like the Cessna Citation M2? Yes. The direct operating cost for the PC12 is noticeably lower. The M2 burns far more fuel and the maintenance is more demanding, but you trade speed for savings. The Citation will take you to your destination almost 100 knots faster. So, while the PC12 is more efficient, it's not always the best choice if time matters more than fuel burn. The real advantage of the PC12 is its versatility. You get a pressurized cabin, long range, solid performance, and the ability to land on short or rough strips that most jets can't touch. That's what makes its operating cost feel reasonable to many owners.

year will result in variable costs 10 times greater than if you were to fly 50 hours per year thus the estimated variable cost for private Jets is presented as an hourly figure this is how much extra Like any high-value asset,&nbsp;&nbsp; a Gulfstream G Seven Hundred loses value&nbsp; over time. Depreciation rates can vary,&nbsp;&nbsp; but private jets typically depreciate at a&nbsp; rate of five to ten percent per year. Over&nbsp;&nbsp; a period of ten years, this can result in a&nbsp; significant loss in the aircraft's value. For&nbsp;&nbsp; a jet that cost nearly one hundred million&nbsp; dollars, this could mean a depreciation loss&nbsp;&nbsp; of five to ten million dollars per year. Depreciation is an inevitable part of owning a&nbsp;&nbsp; private jet, and it’s something potential owners&nbsp; need to consider. While the value of the jet may&nbsp;&nbsp; decrease over time, the costs of maintenance,&nbsp; crew, fuel, and other expenses remain constant,&nbsp;&nbsp; making it a considerable ongoing&nbsp; financial commitment. Additional Expenses: Owning a Gulfstream G Seven Hundred also&nbsp;&nbsp; comes with a host of other potential expenses.&nbsp; Upgrades and modifications are common,&nbsp;&nbsp; especially as new technologies emerge. Whether&nbsp; it's upgrading the avionics, installing new&nbsp;&nbsp; entertainment systems, or refurbishing the&nbsp; interior, these costs can add up quickly. Then there are taxes and registration fees, which&nbsp; vary depending on the country and state where&nbsp;&nbsp; the jet is registered. These can include import&nbsp; taxes, sales taxes, and annual registration fees,&nbsp;&nbsp; which can total anywhere from several thousand to&nbsp; several hundred thousand dollars annually. Finally, there are the costs associated with&nbsp; using private terminals, or Fixed Base Operators.&nbsp;&nbsp; These facilities offer a range of services, from&nbsp; fueling and maintenance to passenger lounges and&nbsp;&nbsp; catering. While Fixed Base Operators provide&nbsp; a high level of convenience and comfort, they&nbsp;&nbsp; also come with additional fees, which can vary&nbsp; depending on the location and services used. After considering all these costs, you&nbsp; might be wondering if owning a Gulfstream&nbsp;&nbsp; G Seven Hundred is worth it. The answer&nbsp; depends on your perspective. For many,&nbsp;&nbsp; the prestige and convenience of owning one&nbsp; of the world's most advanced private jets&nbsp;&nbsp; outweigh the substantial costs. The G Seven&nbsp; Hundred offers unparalleled comfort, speed,&nbsp;&nbsp; and luxury, making it an attractive option for&nbsp; those who value their time and privacy. For business executives, the ability to travel&nbsp; quickly and efficiently between meetings in&nbsp;&nbsp; different parts of the world can be invaluable.&nbsp; For others, the sheer luxury and exclusivity of&nbsp;&nbsp; owning such a jet is a status symbol, a testament&nbsp; to their success and lifestyle. The Gulfstream&nbsp;&nbsp; G Seven Hundred is more than just a mode of&nbsp; transportation; it's a statement, a flying office,&nbsp;&nbsp; and a private sanctuary all rolled into one. Owning a Gulfstream G Seven Hundred is a&nbsp;&nbsp; significant financial commitment&nbsp; that goes far beyond the initial&nbsp;&nbsp; purchase price. The ongoing costs of maintenance,&nbsp; crew salaries, fuel, insurance, depreciation, and&nbsp;&nbsp; other expenses add up quickly. However, for those&nbsp; who can afford it, the benefits of owning such an&nbsp;&nbsp; advanced and luxurious jet are undeniable. Whether you’re considering purchasing a Gulfstream&nbsp;&nbsp; G Seven Hundred or are simply curious about&nbsp; the costs involved, it’s clear that owning one&nbsp;&nbsp; of these magnificent machines is not just about&nbsp; the money; it's about embracing a lifestyle that&nbsp;&nbsp; values convenience, privacy, and unparalleled&nbsp; luxury. So, if you're ready to take the plunge,&nbsp;&nbsp; the G Seven Hundred offers an experience&nbsp; like no other—a true marvel of modern&nbsp;&nbsp; aviation that lets you own the sky. - 3 Things To Watch For When Flying a TBM: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Okay. So, with the propeller, one thing you have to look out for on the TBM, as you can see, it's a very big prop. It's a large prop, but one thing that you have to look out for is, as you can see here, it's approximately sitting at about 10 inches off the the ground, right? So, the tip of the blade is about 10 inches off the ground. So, you can imagine when the propeller is spinning and it's creating thrust, um, it will pull the nose of the aircraft down a little bit, which will bring the prop even closer to the ground. So, one thing you really got to look out for is as you're taxiing, um, you got to make sure that you're not taxiing in an area that has tall blades of grass, um, maybe deep snow or even, um, small rocks or pebbles on the on the runway or the taxi way. because if you do, it's very very easy to chip the prop or get a prop strike in this aircraft. So with that in mind, you can imagine your landing has to be very careful as well. You got to be very careful in your landing because in this aircraft um if you land flat, right? So that means if you land on the mains and your nose wheel at the same time and you have all that weight coming down, um it's very very easy to compress the shocks, right? Which would then even further lower or decrease this distance between the tip of the propeller and the ground. So it's not uncommon to have a prop strike in this airplane on landing just from landing um with an improper landing technique. So landing flat or slightly nose wheel first will probably guarantee a prop strike. So you've got to be very very careful with this aircraft on landing. Um there's a few tips and stabilized approach criteria to meet in order to u avoid that scenario. But you really got to be careful about that. It's a multid-esk uh multiclutch uh type uh well multid-disk multi-ad type um setup. So, they're very powerful, but they also can wear out pretty quickly. financing intricacies surrounding the TBM 960 before diving into the mechanics it's essential to understand the motivations financing such a significant purchase offers a blend of flexibility and financial Prudence by not locking up a considerable sum in a full upfront purchase owners often find the liquidity useful for other investment opportunities or operational needs furthermore in many jurisdictions the interest paid on loans for aircraft acquisition can be tax deductible offering additional Financial benefits the most straightforward financing approach is a traditional loan with terms often spanning from 3 to 20 years buyers can expect down payment requirements ranging from 10 to 20% of the aircraft's value these percentages can translate to substantial amounts given the BBM 960s price point interest rates will vary based on market conditions creditworthiness of the borrower and the terms of the loan currently rates hover in the low to mid singled digigit percentage range but this can fluctuate leasing is another popular route especially for corporations or individuals who prioritize flexibility there are two primary forms this setup allows users to essentially rent the TBM 960 for a predetermined period in rate at the end of the term the aircraft is returned to the Lesser this option is appealing for those who want access to the latest models without the long-term commitment of ownership Additionally the lease payments can often be deducted as operating expenses for tax purposes this is closer to a traditional purchase but spread over time at the end of the lease term the lesie usually has the option to purchase the aircraft at a predetermined residual value this form is beneficial for those who anticipate long-term use of the TBM 960 but prefer the cash flow advantages of making payments over time aircraft like many assets depreciate over time in several countries this depreciation can be leveraged for tax benefits accelerated depreciation schemes might allow the owner to write off a significant portion of the TBM 960s value in the initial years leading to substantial tax savings however it's essential to consult with a tax expert familiar with Aviation specific regulations to navigate this aspect ENT entering the world of aircraft ownership particularly with a gem like the TBM 960 is a significant decision while the Allure of the skies and The Prestige of the ownership experience are undeniable Financial Prudence is crucial by understanding and leveraging the Myriad financing options available prospective owners can ensure that their Aviation dreams are both exhilarating and economically sound whether choosing a traditional loan an operating lease or exploring tax benefits through depreciation the key is to tailor the financing structure to individual needs and financial Landscapes the TBM 960 isn't just an aircraft it's an investment in an elevated lifestyle making informed financing decisions ensures that this investment brings joy for years to come both in the air and on the balance sheet the TBN 960 with its blend of speed range and efficiency presents a compelling proposition for anyone in the market for a high performance turbo Pro it's not just an aircraft it's a statement a testament to one's commitment to Excellence in aviation whether for business or pleasure the TBM 960 promises an unparalleled - How To Buy an Old Private Jet - Citation 501 ISP: Kind: captions Language: en hey guys what's up it's Tom and on this episode we're gonna be talking about the citation 501 this is your buyer's guide and don't forget to stick around to the end till I tell you why you probably shouldn't buy the citation 501. [Music] so a little bit of background the citation 501 was actually the first Small Business Jet that was single pilot certified and produced from 1977 to 1985. it's gone through a lot of iterations and modifications during that time it's actually the precursor to the citation jet or the CJ now there are over 600 501s or 500s that were built and we have about 250 over 250 currently in operation today the average airframe time is about 6 000 hours total time and the average vintage is the year 1980. some standard configurations are a five to six passenger capacity in the cabin there's usually a rear lav compartment or it could also be pushed up to the front the cabin stands about four foot three inches so it's kind of like scooting into a large SUV or a minivan and it's usually configured with the jt15d-1a engines let's talk about performance now they are certified to go above 40 000 hours for altitude but you're probably going to be spending most of your time in the low 30s it boasts a fuel capacity of 3 700 pounds and you're looking at a balanced Runway of about 3 500 to 4 000 feet for both landing and takeoff although I have heard of a citation Pilots getting in and out of 322 knots with about 2719 feet per minute climb rate and about 162 gallons per hour fuel burn some common updates or modifications are thrust reversers with the engines anti-skid breaks that whole package also you're going to be looking at an at the baggage compartment that can fit some additional cargo so that you don't have to put it up in the cabin with you and then of course all the modifications that came out of Uvalde Texas with Sierra Industries most notably that is the extended range fuel tanks and adding the Williams engines to the fuselage if you're looking at a citation 501 you're probably looking at some common programs that you might find out there especially for the engines if you are looking at a stallion or an eagle modification you're going to be looking at the Williams tap program that's the tap blue tab Elite program for the engines or if you're looking at regular engines you can also be looking at the Skyway mro Flex program that will extend the TBO of the engines currently on your jet the other way to go as far as engines go is to find used engines when yours come time for an overhaul all right now let's talk about why you shouldn't buy a citation 501. number one they're older so you're going to have a higher maintenance cost they're going to break a little bit more often that's just the reality of buying buying older jet although it's   5. @MichaelMiller-f6k [1 likes]: Interesting to see your perspective.

I spent YEARS flying these things.  They are fantastic airplanes, built to last, and there are a lot of parts out there.  

Low Utilization Maintenance program really gets your MX costs down a LOT.  Phase 1-4 every 3 years, Phase 1-5 every 6 years (I think they’ve even pushed that out to 8 now).  

Stay away from “big name” shops, they like to replace stuff that doesn’t need replacing, charge WAY more for labor, and generally take your money in the name of “safety” which isn’t really the case.  If you are part 91, engine overhaul doesn’t need to happen…..HSI and move on.  The entire history of the entire 500 series, there has been ONE catastrophic failure of the P & W JT-6 engine.  The darn thing is bulletproof and will run forever if you properly maintain it and run it right.  Pull back your ITT’s about 40 degrees in cruise…..you’ll lose 5-10 knots of speed and save $100k when your HSI comes around.  

The MX “gotchas” are few, but they do exist:  

Nitrogen blow down bottle:  $10k  
Fire bottles in then engines 
Landing gear system check (every 1000 landings, can be a six figure expense if you are in and out of short runways all the time and are banging it in and riding brakes hard.  Thrust reversers help on this a lot).  

Battery is $3800 bucks (there is a $3100 one but get the expensive one:  it’s higher amps and will start your engines cooler.  HSI time will be much less painful with that.  

Start your engines on a power cart every chance you get.  Saves your battery AND starts cooler.  Really helps your engine life.  Most decent FBO’s will throw it in for free if you buy gas.  

Insurance for me is $8200 bucks a year with $1mm liability and $500k hull.  Goes up with more hull and less experience.  (I’m an ATP with 10,000 hours of turbine and 1,200 in type).  Can get expensive with less experience, ESPECIALLY single pilot.  

No issues getting into the mid to high 30’s in the bird….itll do it all day long.  Can get your fuel burn down to 600 lbs per hour there at about 310-320 knots.  

GREAT airplane.   Cheap to operate (relatively speaking) for a jet.  Slow, but perfectly comfortable and adequate.  Yeah, you can buy a CJ or a Mustang for $2mm but you can buy a good 501SP for $500k.  $1.5mm buys a hell of a lot of gas and maintenance.
  6. @dwighttaylor5107 [1 likes]: Awesome thanks
  7. @annehechesliverdonation9624 [1 likes]: one of the biggest money pits around
  8. @lorenjackson8961 [1 likes]: Yeah...wait until you have to replace the emergency landing gear extention nitrogen bottle. They're no longer able to be static tested after 24 or 25 years I think. They are like $10,000 dollars each and they are nearly impossible to find.

of hours that the aircraft has flown therefore the more the aircraft flies the more maintenance it will require however this cost also takes into account any unexpected maintenance events for example - Pilatus PC-24 vs HondaJet Elite II: Ultimate Light Jet Comparison!: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] The Pus PC 24 and the Honda Jet Elite 2. These jets are game changers in their class, but which one is the better fit for your mission? Whether you're an owner pilot, a business traveler, or just love private jets, stick around as we break down their design, performance, cabins, avionics, and costs. Let's get started. First, let's get to know these jets. The Pus PC24 built by Swiss manufacturer Polus Aircraft is marketed as the super versatile jet. Launched in 2018, it's the first business jet from a company renowned for its rugged PC12 turborop. The PC24 is designed to combine jet performance with the ability to operate on short, unpaved runways, a unique feature in this category. On the other side, we have the Honda Jet Elite 2. The latest iteration from Honda Aircraft Company, released in 2022. Known for its sleek design and innovative over-the-wing engine mount, the Honda Jet Elite 2 builds on the success of the original Honda Jet, offering improved range, efficiency, and tech. It's a favorite among owner pilots and small charter operations. Both jets target the light jet market, but their design philosophies differ. The PC24 is all about versatility and ruggedness, while the Honda Jet Elite 2 emphasizes efficiency and style. Let's dive into the details. Performance is where these jets start to show their strengths. The latest PC24 is powered by two Williams International FJ44-4A turboan engines, delivering a maximum thrust of 3,420 lb each. It boasts a top speed of 440 knots, around 506 mph at 28,000 ft and a maximum range of 2,000 nautical miles with four passengers. Its service ceiling is an impressive 45,000 ft, allowing it to cruise above most weather. What sets the PC24 apart is its ability to operate on unpaved runways like grass or gravel with a takeoff distance of just 2,930 ft at maximum takeoff weight 18,300 lb. This makes it ideal for remote destinations where other jets can't go. The Honda Jet Elite 2, powered by 2G Honda HF120 turbo fans with 2,50 lbs of thrust each, is slightly slower with a top speed of 422 knots, about 486 mph. However, it shines in range, offering up to 1547 nautical miles with four passengers, though some sources note its real world range is closer to 1,400 nautical miles with full seats. Its service ceiling is 43,000 ft. Still plenty high for smooth flights. The Honda Jet requires a longer runway with a takeoff distance of about 4,000 ft at its maximum takeoff weight of 11,100 lb. It's designed for paved runways, so it's less versatile than the PC24 in terms of airport access. In terms of raw performance, the PC24 is faster and can access more diverse runways. The Honda Jet's lighterweight and efficient engines make it a strong contender for standard operations. If you need to land on a dirt strip, the PC24 is your only choice here. Let's step inside. The Palatus PC24 is a standout in cabin size for a light jet. It offers 501 cubic feet of cabin volume with a height of 5.08 ft, width of 5.58 ft, and length of 23 ft. This spacious cabin can seat six to eight passengers in an executive layout or up to 10 in a commuter configuration. Its flat floor and large rear cargo door 51 in wide by 49 in tall make it easy to reconfigure for passengers, cargo or even medevac missions. The PC24 also includes 90 cubic feet of internal baggage space accessible in flight which is a big plus for long trips. However, frequent reconfiguration might lead to wear and tear on the interior. So, keep that in mind if luxury is your priority. The Honda Jet Elite 2, by contrast, has a smaller cabin at 324 cubic feet with a height of 4.83 ft, width of 5.0 ft, and length of 17.8 ft., it typically seats four to six passengers in a club seating arrangement with an optional side-facing seat. The cabin is sleek and modern with a focus on comfort for shorter missions. Baggage space is more limited with 66 cubic feet split between a nose and aft compartment and no in-flight access. The Honda Jet's compact cabin is cozy, which might feel cramped for taller passengers or longer flights. The PC 24 wins hands down for cabin space and versatility, especially if you need to carry more passengers or cargo. But the Honda Jet Elite 2 offers a refined, intimate cabin that's perfect for smaller groups on shorter trips. In the cockpit, the Pus PC24 features the advanced cockpit environment. Built around the Honeywell Primus Epic 2.0 avionic suite, it includes four 12-in displays, synthetic vision, auto throttle, cast 2, and LPV approach capability. The PC24 is certified for single pilot operations, making it appealing for owner pilots. The Honda Jet Elite 2 counters with a Garmin G3000 avionic suite tailored for its needs. It features three 14-in displays, touchscreen controllers, and advanced features like auto throttle, synthetic vision, and surface watch for runway safety. Like the PC24, it's single pilot certified, but its intuitive Garmin system is a favorite among pilots for ease of use. Both jets offer cuttingedge avionics, but the Honda Jet's Garmin G3000 feels more modern and userfriendly, while the PC24's Honeywell suite is robust and proven. It's a close call, but pilot preference might tip the scales here. Let's talk about money. The Pus PC24 has a base price of around $10.7 million as of 2025, though fully equipped models can reach $12 million. Operating costs vary, but estimates suggest around $2,500 per flight hour, factoring in fuel, maintenance, and crew. Its rugged design and Williams engines keep maintenance costs competitive, but its larger size means higher fuel burn. The Honda Jet Elite 2 is more affordable with a base price of about $6.95 million, though options can push it closer to $7.5 million. Operating costs are lower, around $1,800 per flight hour, thanks to its efficient HF120 engines and lighter airframe. For budget conscious buyers, the Honda Jet is the clear winner. The PC24's higher price and operating costs reflect its larger size and unique capabilities. If you're looking for value and efficiency, the Honda Jet Elite 2 is hard to beat. The PC24 has carved out a niche since its 2018 debut with over 100 units delivered by 2025. Its versatility appeals to operators needing access to remote locations from corporate travelers to medevac services. However, its higher price and newer design mean it's still building brand loyalty against established players like Cessna and Ember. The Honda Jet Elite 2 benefits from a loyal owner pilot base with over 250 Honda Jets delivered since the original model. Its reputation for reliability and low operating costs makes it a favorite for short to medium range missions. Though its smaller cabin limits its appeal for larger groups. So, which jet comes out on top? The Pus PC24 is the king of versatility with a spacious cabin, unmatched runway performance, and long range. It's perfect for those who need a do-it-all jet from remote landings to executive travel. The Honda Jet Elite 2, meanwhile, excels in efficiency, affordability, and style, making it ideal for owner pilots or smaller groups on shorter missions. If you prioritize cabin space and rugged capability, go for the PC24. If budget, efficiency, and a sleek design are your focus, the Honda Jet Elite 2 is your jet. Either way, you're getting a top tier light jet. What do you think? Would you pick the rugged PC 24 or the sleek Honda Jet Elite 2? Drop your thoughts in the comments, and don't forget to like, subscribe, and hit that bell for more aviation content. Until next time, fly safe.
